html:
[403]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 人主何色不可致 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why can't the ruler have any beauty he desires?'
  • 眷一妇 - Literal meaning 'favor one woman,' but implies an excessive or irrational attachment.
  • 痴甚矣 - Judgmental phrase meaning 'extremely foolish' or 'madly foolish.'
  • 流水闝 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'a man who is fickle or easily swayed by emotions,' derived from the image of flowing water.
  • 争风闝 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'a man who is obsessed with romantic rivalry or jealousy,' derived from the image of competing for beauty.
  • 致丧国 - Means 'led to the downfall of the state,' emphasizing the severe consequences of their actions.
  • 忍冷以归 - Literally 'endure the cold and return home,' but implies a sense of self-restraint or humility.
  • 闻其负冷 - Means 'hearing that she is suffering from the cold,' but could also imply a deeper emotional connection.
  • 伤其心 - Means 'hurt her heart,' but in this context, suggests a more profound emotional impact.
  • 万一致疾 - Hypothetical phrase meaning 'even if it leads to illness,' emphasizing the potential negative consequences.
  • 貌劣而善媚 - Means 'ugly but good at flattery,' describing a person who compensates for physical unattractiveness with charm.
  • 候桶暖 - Means 'wait until the bucket is warm,' implying a deliberate and considerate action.
  • 欣然每夸于人 - Means 'joyfully and often boast to others,' highlighting the poet's pride in his experience.
  • 奇遇 - Means 'a rare and fortunate encounter,' emphasizing the uniqueness of the situation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宋子京 - Historical figure, likely referring to Song Zian, a prominent statesman and scholar during the Northern Song Dynasty.
  • 锦江 - Famous river in Chengdu, known for its scenic beauty and cultural significance.
  • 王衍 - Historical figure, likely referring to Wang Yan, a politician and calligrapher from the late Tang Dynasty.
  • 宋道君 - Historical figure, likely referring to Emperor Huizong of Song, known for his artistic and cultural pursuits.
  • 荀奉倩 - Historical figure, likely referring to Xun Fengqian, a scholar and writer from the Eastern Han Dynasty.
  • 吴下 - Term referring to the region around modern-day Suzhou and Hangzhou, known for its cultural and literary heritage.
  • 金儿 - Historical figure, likely referring to a famous courtesan from the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 陈体方 - Historical figure, likely referring to Chen Tifang, a poet and scholar from the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 黄秀云 - Historical figure, likely referring to a courtesan or a woman of high social standing.

Proper Nouns

  • 宋子京 - Song Zian
  • 锦江 - Jinjiang River
  • 王衍 - Wang Yan
  • 宋道君 - Emperor Huizong of Song
  • 荀奉倩 - Xun Fengqian
  • 吴下 - Wuxia (region around Suzhou and Hangzhou)
  • 金儿 - Jin'er (famous courtesan)
  • 陈体方 - Chen Tifang
  • 黄秀云 - Huang Xiuyun

[404]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 体方死而有知，犹必吟完百首 - If Ti Fang were to die and retain consciousness, he would still recite a hundred poems.
  • 秀云死亦必相从 - Xiu Yun would also surely follow, implying that Xiu Yun would join Ti Fang in death.
  • 不然，体方亦必以赖婚讼于地下主者 - Otherwise, Ti Fang would also surely sue the underground ruler for a broken marriage promise.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 体方 - A proper name of an individual in the story.
  • 秀云 - A proper name of an individual in the story.
  • 洛阳王某 - A proper name, referring to a man from Luoyang.
  • 周府郡王者，人称鼓楼东殿下 - A proper title, referring to a nobleman from the Zhou family, known as the 'Prince of the East Drum Tower.'
  • 唐玉簪 - A proper name, referring to a courtesan.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '体方死而有知，犹必吟完百首' is elliptical and requires careful parsing. The main structure is 'If Ti Fang were to die and retain consciousness, he would still recite a hundred poems.'
  • The sentence '秀云死亦必相从' is a straightforward conditional statement, but it relies on the context to fully understand the relationship between Ti Fang and Xiu Yun.
  • The sentence '不然，体方亦必以赖婚讼于地下主者' is more complex and requires careful interpretation. The main structure is 'Otherwise, Ti Fang would also surely sue the underground ruler for a broken marriage promise.'

[405]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 相爱，本以为欢也 - To love each other, originally thinking it would be joy.
  • 既净身矣，安用见为？ - Since the body is already pure, what need is there for seeing?
  • 噫！是乃所以为情也 - Ah! This is what is called emotion.
  • 夫情近于淫，而淫实非情 - Emotion is close to lust, but lust is not truly emotion.
  • 今纵欲之夫，获新而置旧 - Nowadays, men who indulge in desire, they gain the new and discard the old.
  • 妒色之妇，因婢而虐夫 - Jealous women, because of their maids, abuse their husbands.
  • 情安在乎？ - Where is the emotion?
  • 惟淫心未除故耳 - Only because the lustful heart has not been removed.
  • 不留他人余欢之地，而专以一见为快 - Not leaving room for others' leftover joy, but solely seeking pleasure in one encounter.
  • 此一见时，有无穷之情 - In this one encounter, there is infinite emotion.
  • 此一见后，更无余情 - After this one encounter, there is no more emotion left.
  • 情之所极，乃至相死而不悔 - The extreme of emotion, even to the point of dying together without regret.
  • 虽然，谓之情则可，谓之非痴则不可 - Although, calling it emotion is acceptable, calling it not foolish is not acceptable.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 樂和 - The name of the author or narrator.
  • 南宋 - Refers to the Southern Song Dynasty (1127–1279), a period in Chinese history.
  • 臨安 - The capital city of the Southern Song Dynasty, now known as Hangzhou.
  • 錢塘門 - A gate in the city walls of Hangzhou.
  • 衣冠之族 - Refers to the gentry or literati class, typically educated and of higher social status.
  • 舅家 - Refers to the maternal family, where the child is raised.
  • 永清巷 - A specific street or neighborhood in Hangzhou.
  • 喜将士 - Refers to a person who likes military affairs or is a military enthusiast.
  • 順娘 - The name of the woman, likely a common name in the context.
  • 喜樂和順，合是天緣 - A play on words using the names of the characters, suggesting a heavenly match or fated relationship.

Proper Nouns

  • 樂和 - The name of the author or narrator.
  • 南宋 - The Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 臨安 - Hangzhou.
  • 錢塘門 - A gate in Hangzhou.
  • 衣冠之族 - The gentry or literati class.
  • 舅家 - Maternal family.
  • 永清巷 - A specific street or neighborhood in Hangzhou.
  • 喜将士 - A military enthusiast.
  • 順娘 - The name of the woman.
  • 喜樂和順，合是天緣 - A play on words suggesting a fated relationship.

[406]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 久之馆散 - Colloquial expression indicating the passage of time and the dispersal of people or objects.
  • 各不相闻 - Means 'each did not hear from the other,' indicating a lack of communication.
  • 值清明节 - '值' means 'coinciding with' or 'on,' and '清明节' is the Qingming Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday for tomb sweeping.
  • 舅家邀甥扫墓 - '舅家' refers to the maternal uncle's family, and '邀甥扫墓' means 'invited the nephew to sweep the tombs.'
  • 因便游湖 - '因便' means 'taking the opportunity,' and '游湖' means 'to tour the lake.'
  • 杭俗湖船男女不避 - '杭俗' refers to the customs of Hangzhou, and '湖船男女不避' means 'in lake boats, men and women do not avoid each other.'
  • 适喜家宅眷亦出游 - '适' means 'just then' or 'happened to,' and '喜家宅眷' refers to the family of the person named Xi, and '亦出游' means 'also went out.'
  • 会于一船 - Means 'met on one boat.'
  • 姿态发越 - Means 'her appearance and demeanor were radiant.'
  • 魂消 - Means 'his soul was captivated' or 'he was enchanted.'
  • 一揖之外 - Means 'beyond a bow.'
  • 微微送笑 - Means 'gave a slight smile.'
  • 题绝句于桃花笺云 - '题绝句' means 'wrote a quatrain,' and '桃花笺' means 'on peach blossom paper.'
  • 嫩蕊娇香郁未开 - Describes a flower bud that is delicate, fragrant, and yet to bloom.
  • 不因蜂蝶自生猜 - Means 'not because of bees and butterflies does it naturally invite suspicion.'
  • 他年若作扁舟侣 - '他年' means 'one day in the future,' and '若作扁舟侣' means 'if we become companions in a small boat.'
  • 日日西湖一醉回 - Means 'we will return to the West Lake every day, drunk.'
  • 题毕，折为方胜 - '题毕' means 'after finishing writing,' and '折为方胜' means 'folded it into a square charm.'
  • 明日携至永清巷 - '明日' means 'the next day,' and '携至永清巷' means 'carried it to Yongqing Lane.'
  • 徘徊数次 - Means 'hesitated several times.'
  • 闻潮王庙著灵 - '闻' means 'heard,' and '潮王庙著灵' means 'the temple of Emperor Chao was known for its spiritual power.'
  • 私市香烛祷焉 - '私市' means 'privately bought,' and '香烛祷焉' means 'incense and candles for prayer.'
  • 焚楮之际 - '焚楮' means 'burning paper money,' and '之际' means 'at that moment.'
  • 袖中方胜偶坠火中 - '袖中' means 'in his sleeve,' and '偶坠火中' means 'accidentally fell into the fire.'
  • 急简之 - Means 'quickly picked it up.'
  • 已烬 - Means 'it was already burned.'
  • 惟余一侣字 - '惟余' means 'only remained,' and '一侣字' means 'the character '侣.'
  • 侣者双口 - Means 'the character '侣' consists of two mouths.'
  • 和自以为吉征也 - '和' is the name of the person, and '自以为吉征也' means 'he believed it was a good omen.'
  • 步入碑亭 - Means 'walked into the stele pavilion.'
  • 方凝思间 - '方' means 'just,' and '凝思间' means 'in the midst of deep thought.'
  • 忽见一老叟 - '忽见' means 'suddenly saw,' and '老叟' means 'an old man.'
  • 衣冠甚古 - Means 'dressed very anciently.'
  • 手握团扇，上写姻缘二字 - '手握团扇' means 'holding a round fan,' and '上写姻缘二字' means 'with the characters '姻缘' (fate and marriage) written on it.'
  • 和问曰 - '和问曰' means 'He asked.'
  • 翁能算姻缘之事乎 - '翁' means 'elder,' and '能算姻缘之事乎' means 'can you calculate matters of fate and marriage?'
  • 叟云 - '叟云' means 'the old man said.'
  • 因询年甲 - '因询' means 'because he asked,' and '年甲' means 'age.'
  • 于五指上轮算良久 - '于五指上轮算' means 'calculated using his fingers,' and '良久' means 'for a long time.'
  • 佳眷是熟人，非生人也 - '佳眷' means 'good spouse,' and '是熟人，非生人也' means 'is a familiar person, not a stranger.'
  • 某正拟一熟人，未审缘法如何 - '某' means 'I,' and '正拟一熟人，未审缘法如何' means 'I am planning to marry a familiar person, but I do not know the fate of our relationship.'
  • 叟引至八角井边 - '叟引至' means 'the old man led him to,' and '八角井边' means 'the side of the octagonal well.'
  • 使和视井中有缘与否 - '使和视' means 'made him look,' and '井中有缘与否' means 'whether there is a connection in the well.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 清明节 - The Qingming Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday for tomb sweeping and honoring ancestors.
  • 舅家 - Refers to the maternal uncle's family.
  • 杭俗 - Refers to the customs of Hangzhou, a city known for its scenic beauty and cultural heritage.
  • 湖船男女不避 - Reflects the social norms of the time, where men and women could interact more freely in certain settings.
  • 桃花笺 - Peach blossom paper, a type of decorative paper often used for writing poetry.
  • 潮王庙 - The temple of Emperor Chao, a local deity known for his spiritual power.
  • 香烛 - Incense and candles, commonly used in religious and spiritual practices.
  • 焚楮 - Burning paper money, a traditional practice to honor the dead.
  • 方胜 - A square charm, often used in traditional Chinese culture for good luck.
  • 八角井 - An octagonal well, a common feature in traditional Chinese architecture.

[407]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 和見井内水勢汹涌，如万顷汪洋，其明如镜 - Describes the well's water as surging like a vast ocean, clear as a mirror.
  • 中有美女，年可十六七，紫罗杏黄裙，绰约可爱 - Describes a beautiful woman inside the well, aged around sixteen or seventeen, wearing a purple silk and apricot yellow skirt, graceful and lovely.
  • 细辨，乃顺娘也 - Upon closer inspection, it is identified as Shun娘.
  • 喜极往就，不觉坠井，惊觉乃梦耳 - Overjoyed, he went to her, only to fall into the well, realizing it was a dream upon waking.
  • 查碑文：其神石瑰，唐时捐财筑塘捍水，没为潮王 - Investigating the stone inscription, it states that the spirit is Shi Gui, who in the Tang dynasty donated money to build a dike to protect against floods and was later deified as the King of Tides.
  • 和意梦中所见叟即神也 - He believed the old man he saw in the dream was the deity.
  • 还告诸父，欲往请婚 - He told his parents and wanted to go to ask for their daughter's hand in marriage.
  • 父谓盛衰势殊，徒取其怒 - His father said that their fortunes were vastly different, and it would only provoke anger.
  • 再请舅，舅亦不许 - He then asked his uncle, who also refused.
  • 和大失望，乃纸书牌位，供亲妻喜顺娘 - Disappointed, he wrote out a tablet and set up an altar for his deceased wife, Xishun娘.
  • 昼则对食，夜置枕旁，三唤而后寝 - During the day, he would eat facing the altar; at night, he would place the tablet beside his pillow and call out three times before sleeping.
  • 每至胜节佳会，必整容出访，绝无一遇 - Whenever there were grand festivals or gatherings, he would dress up and go out, but never met anyone.
  • 有议婚者，和坚谢之，誓必俟顺娘嫁后乃可 - When others proposed marriage, he firmly declined, vowing to wait until Shun娘 was married.
  • 而顺娘亦竟蹉跎未字 - Shun娘 also remained unmarried.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 井内水势汹涌 - The well's water surging, which could be interpreted as a supernatural phenomenon.
  • 顺娘 - A name that might have cultural significance, possibly referring to a historical figure or a character in folklore.
  • 神石瑰 - The deity Shi Gui, who was deified in the Tang dynasty for his contributions to flood control.
  • 唐时捐财筑塘捍水 - Refers to the historical practice of building dikes to protect against floods, a common practice in ancient China.
  • 潮王 - The King of Tides, a deity associated with water and floods.
  • 胜节佳会 - Refers to grand festivals or important gatherings, which were significant social events in ancient China.

Proper Nouns

  • 和 - The name of the protagonist.
  • 顺娘 - The name of the woman in the well, possibly a historical figure or a character in folklore.
  • 神石瑰 - The name of the deity Shi Gui.
  • 唐 - The Tang dynasty, a historical period in China.
  • 潮王 - The King of Tides, a deity associated with water and floods.
  • 胜节佳会 - Grand festivals or important gatherings.

[408]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 又三年，八月 - Indicates a specific time frame, 'three years later, in August.'
  • 因觀潮之會 - Means 'taking advantage of the tide-watching gathering,' referring to a traditional event where people gather to watch the tides.
  • 推身人叢 - Means 'pushed through the crowd,' describing the action of moving through a dense group of people.
  • 漸逼視之 - Means 'gradually got closer to look at her,' indicating a slow approach to observe something.
  • 順娘亦覺 - Means 'Shunniang also noticed,' suggesting mutual awareness.
  • 交相注目 - Means 'looked at each other intently,' describing a mutual gaze.
  • 忽聞喧言潮至 - Means 'suddenly heard the loud announcement that the tide was coming,' indicating a sudden and loud announcement.
  • 眾俱散走 - Means 'everyone scattered and ran away,' describing a chaotic reaction to the approaching tide.
  • 潮勢甚猛 - Means 'the tide was very powerful,' emphasizing the strength of the tide.
  • 如水城數丈 - Means 'like a wall of water several zhang high,' using a vivid metaphor to describe the height of the water.
  • 順娘失足墜於潮中 - Means 'Shunniang lost her footing and fell into the tide,' describing the accident.
  • 和驟見哀痛 - Means 'He suddenly saw and was filled with grief,' expressing a sudden emotional reaction.
  • 意不相舍 - Means 'he did not want to leave her,' indicating a strong desire to stay with someone.
  • 仓皇逐之 - Means 'rushed after her in a panic,' describing a hurried and anxious pursuit.
  • 弄潮子弟 - Means 'young men who play with the tides,' referring to local youth who engage in tide-watching and other related activities.
  • 競往捞救 - Means 'rushed to rescue her,' indicating a collective effort to save someone.
  • 紫罗衫杏黄裙 - Refers to 'a purple silk robe and an apricot yellow skirt,' describing Shunniang's clothing.
  • 二尸对面相抱 - Means 'the two bodies were facing each other and hugging,' describing their position after being rescued.
  • 唤之不苏 - Means 'calling them did not revive them,' indicating that they were unresponsive.
  • 拆之亦不解 - Means 'trying to separate them did not work,' suggesting that they were tightly bound together.
  • 乐翁闻儿变 - Means 'the father of the child heard about his daughter's situation,' indicating a family member's reaction.
  • 跄攘而至 - Means 'hurriedly arrived,' describing a rushed arrival.
  • 哭曰 - Means 'cried and said,' indicating an emotional outburst.
  • 儿生不得吹箫侣，死当成连理枝耳 - Means 'My daughter, who could not find a flute-playing partner in life, will become a couple in death,' expressing a poignant wish.
  • 恚曰 - Means 'angrily said,' indicating a strong emotional response.
  • 何不早言，悔之何及 - Means 'Why didn't you say something earlier, it's too late to regret now,' expressing frustration and regret.
  • 今若再活，当遂其愿也 - Means 'If they are revived now, we will fulfill their wish,' indicating a decision to proceed.
  • 高声共唤 - Means 'shouted together to call them,' describing a collective effort to revive them.
  • 逾时始苏 - Means 'after a while, they finally revived,' indicating the passage of time before revival.
  • 毫无困状 - Means 'showed no signs of distress,' indicating a smooth recovery.
  • 若有神佑焉 - Means 'as if protected by a divine force,' suggesting a supernatural element.
  • 喜公不敢负诺 - Means 'Xigong did not dare to break his promise,' indicating a commitment to fulfill a vow.
  • 择日婚配 - Means 'chose a day for the marriage,' indicating a formal arrangement.
  • 事见《小说》 - Means 'the story is recorded in a novel,' indicating the source of the narrative.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 观潮之会 - Refers to the traditional Chinese practice of tide-watching, often associated with coastal regions and local festivals.
  • 弄潮子弟 - Refers to local youth who engage in tide-watching and other related activities, often seen as a form of entertainment or daredevilry.
  • 紫罗衫杏黄裙 - Describes traditional Chinese clothing, with '紫罗衫' (purple silk robe) and '杏黄裙' (apricot yellow skirt) being specific colors and materials.
  • 连理枝 - A metaphor for a harmonious and inseparable couple, often used in Chinese literature and poetry.
  • 《小说》 - Refers to a collection of short stories or novels, indicating the source of the narrative.

Proper Nouns

  • 和 - The name of the male character.
  • 顺娘 - The name of the female character.
  • 喜氏 - The surname of the family.
  • 乐翁 - The name of the father.

[409]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一对多情 - Colloquial phrase meaning 'many lovers' or 'multiple romantic attachments.'
  • 若非得潮神撮合 - 'If not for the intercession of the tide god,' where '撮合' means 'to mediate' or 'to bring together.'
  • 且为情死矣 - 'Would die for love,' a hyperbolic expression emphasizing deep emotional commitment.
  • 期于梁 - 'Agreed to meet at the bridge,' where '期' means 'to agree to meet' and '梁' is a bridge.
  • 水至不去 - 'Would not leave even when the water came,' indicating steadfastness in the face of danger.
  • 抱梁柱而死 - 'Died by hugging the bridge pillar,' a tragic act of loyalty.
  • 万世情痴之祖 - 'The ancestor of eternal love痴,' where '情痴' means 'obsessed with love' or 'madly in love.'
  • 好狎游 - 'Likes to frolic and play,' where '狎游' means 'to play freely' or 'to frolic.'
  • 常为倡家营办生业 - 'Often helps the brothel owner manage business,' where '倡家' refers to a brothel owner and '营办生业' means 'to manage business.'
  • 绸缪 - 'Intimate,' a term often used to describe close relationships.
  • 约窃负而逃 - 'Planned to run away secretly,' where '窃负' means 'to secretly carry away' or 'to run away.'
  • 羞愧不食 - 'Too ashamed to eat,' indicating deep embarrassment.
  • 同窜去 - 'Fled together,' where '窜' means 'to flee.'
  • 今不复可询所在也 - 'Now cannot be asked about their whereabouts,' indicating that the couple has disappeared.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 尾生 - A legendary figure known for his loyalty and tragic end, often cited as an example of unwavering devotion.
  • 傅七郎 - A historical or fictional character, likely a commoner or servant.
  • 蕲春 - A place name, possibly a town or region in China.
  • 散乐林小姐 - 'Scattered entertainment Lin Miss,' likely referring to a performer or courtesan named Lin.
  • 淳熙十六年九月 - A specific date in the Chinese calendar, indicating the time period of the event.
  • 绍熙三年春 - Another specific date in the Chinese calendar, indicating a later time period.
  • 秦州 - A place name, possibly a city or region in China.
  • 当垆 - A term referring to serving at a tavern, often associated with the story of Sima Xiangru and Zhuo Wenjun.
  • 苏客 - 'Su guest,' likely a traveler or visitor.

Proper Nouns

  • 尾生 - A legendary figure.
  • 傅七郎 - A historical or fictional character.
  • 蕲春 - A place name.
  • 散乐林小姐 - A name, likely referring to a performer or courtesan.
  • 傅九 - A name, likely referring to Fu Jiu.
  • 苏客 - A name, likely referring to a traveler or visitor.
  • 李氏 - A family name, likely referring to the R family.

[410]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 相传 - Means 'it is said' or 'according to tradition,' introducing a narrative.
  • 大辟 - Refers to the death penalty, a legal term in classical Chinese.
  • 愚甚 - Means 'very foolish' or 'extremely stupid,' describing the condemned man's character.
  • 刽子 - Refers to an executioner, a specific role in ancient Chinese society.
  • 绐之曰 - Means 'deceived him and said,' indicating the executioner's deceitful speech.
  • 俟午刻流星起时 - Means 'when the meteor rises at noon,' a metaphorical reference to a specific time.
  • 解汝缚 - Means 'unfasten your bonds,' referring to the act of freeing the man.
  • 疾奔远去 - Means 'run quickly and go far away,' describing the man's flight.
  • 囊数金 - Means 'carry several gold pieces,' referring to the man's attempt to repay the executioner.
  • 叩其门 - Means 'knock on his door,' describing the man's visit.
  • 魂也 - Means 'a spirit,' referring to the man's ghostly appearance.
  • 灭矣 - Means 'vanished,' indicating the man's disappearance.
  • 一点破则散矣 - Means 'if one point is broken, it disperses,' a metaphor for the fragility of a spirit.
  • 傅与林苦于防闲 - Means 'Fu and Lin are troubled by being confined,' referring to their situation as spirits.
  • 当垆共役 - Means 'serve together at the wine shop,' describing their shared task.
  • 死贤于生矣 - Means 'death is better than life,' a philosophical statement.
  • 虽谓之不痴可也 - Means 'even if one calls them not foolish, they are,' a sarcastic remark.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 事孰大于继祠承祧者 - Rhetorical question meaning 'What matter is greater than continuing the ancestral sacrifices and inheriting the family line?'
  • 斩艾血胤以媚二淫 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to cut off the bloodline and sacrifice it to please the two debauched women.' '斩艾' is a metaphor for destroying the lineage, and '血胤' refers to the bloodline or descendants.
  • 其心死矣 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'their hearts are dead,' indicating a state of moral corruption or despair.
  • 假令收宫中美人子，卵而翼之，如刘皇后之于李宸妃，异日其子为君，犹未必仇赵氏也 - Complex sentence meaning 'Even if they had taken the sons of beautiful concubines from the palace, raised them like eggs under a wing, as Liu Empress did with Li Xianfei, in the future when their son became emperor, he might not necessarily hold a grudge against the Zhao family.'
  • 自绝其根，身名俱丧，不惟成帝痴，二淫者亦大痴矣 - Concluding statement meaning 'By cutting off their own roots, they lost both their lives and their reputations. Not only was Emperor Cheng foolish, but the two debauched women were also greatly foolish.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 王寵褒姒 - The king favored Bao Si, where *寵* means 'to favor' or 'to indulge in affection.'
  • 廢申后及太子宜臼 - 'Deposed Queen Shen and the crown prince Yijiu,' where *廢* means 'to depose' or 'to remove from position.'
  • 立褒姒為后 - 'Established Bao Si as queen,' where *立* means 'to appoint' or 'to set as.'
  • 以其子伯服為太子 - 'And made her son Bo Fu the crown prince,' where *以* is a causal conjunction meaning 'by making' or 'by appointing.'
  • 褒姒好聞裂缯聲 - 'Bao Si liked to hear the sound of silk being torn,' where *好聞* is 'liked to hear' and *裂缯聲* is the sound of silk being split.
  • 王發缯日裂之 - 'The king would tear the silk daily as he pulled it out,' where *發* is 'to pull out' and *日裂之* is 'daily tearing it.'
  • 以適其意 - 'In order to please her,' where *適* means 'to suit' or 'to satisfy.'
  • 褒姒不好笑 - 'Bao Si did not like to laugh,' where *不好* is 'did not like.'
  • 幽王欲其笑，誘之萬方 - 'King You tried all sorts of ways to make her laugh,' where *誘之萬方* is a set phrase meaning 'to entice in a thousand ways.'
  • 有寇至，舉烽火為信 - 'If enemies came, raise the beacon fire as a signal,' where *舉烽火* is 'to raise the beacon fire' and *為信* is 'as a sign' or 'as a signal.'
  • 兵莫至 - 'No troops came,' where *莫至* is 'none came.'
  • 遂殺幽王骊山下 - 'And so killed King You at Lishan,' where *遂* means 'therefore' or 'thus,' and *骊山* is the location Lishan.
  • 虏褒姒，尽取周赂而去 - 'Captured Bao Si, took all the Zhou treasures, and left,' where *虏* is 'captured' and *尽取* is 'took all of.'
  • 宾媚人一笑，几亡其国 - 'A single laugh from Bin Meiren nearly destroyed her state,' where *几亡* is 'nearly destroyed.'
  • 褒姒一笑，几亡天下 - 'Bao Si’s single laugh nearly destroyed the world,' where *天下* is 'the world' or 'the realm.'
  • 从来笑祸无大于此 - 'From ancient times, no calamity caused by laughter was greater than this,' where *从来* is 'since ancient times' and *笑祸* is 'calamity from laughter.'
  • 齐顷以媚其母 - 'Prince Qi Qing flattered his mother,' where *媚* is 'to flatter' or 'to please.'
  • 而周幽以媚其宠人 - 'But King You flattered his favorite concubine,' where *宠人* is 'favorite person' or 'concubine.'
  • 故幽竟见杀 - 'So King You was eventually killed,' where *见杀* is 'was killed' (passive construction).
  • 而顷卒吊死问疾，以兴其国 - 'But Prince Qi Qing eventually mourned the dead and visited the sick, thus reviving his state,' where *吊死问疾* is 'mourned the dead and visited the sick' and *兴其国* is 'revived his state.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 周幽王 - King You of Zhou, the last king of the Western Zhou dynasty, known for his infidelity and the fall of the Zhou dynasty.
  • 褒姒 - Bao Si, the concubine of King You of Zhou, famous for her beauty and the 'Beacon Fire Farce.'
  • 申后 - Queen Shen, the wife of King You of Zhou and mother of the legitimate heir, Crown Prince Yijiu.
  • 太子宜臼 - Crown Prince Yijiu, the legitimate heir of King You of Zhou, later King Ping of Zhou.
  • 伯服 - Bo Fu, the son of Bao Si, who was made crown prince by King You.
  • 申侯 - The father of Queen Shen, who later rebelled against King You.
  • 鄫人 - The people of Zeng, an ancient state in the Shandong region.
  • 西夷犬戎 - The Western夷 (barbarian) tribes, specifically the Quanrong, a nomadic group from the west.
  • 骊山 - Lishan, a mountain near the ancient capital of the Zhou dynasty, where King You was killed.
  • 宾媚人 - A figure from the *Zuo Zhuan* (Commentary of Zuo), referring to a minister or official whose laughter nearly caused the downfall of his state.
  • 齐顷 - Prince Qi Qing, a historical figure from the state of Qi, known for his filial piety and virtue.

Proper Nouns

  • 周幽王 - King You of Zhou, ruler of the Western Zhou dynasty.
  • 褒姒 - Bao Si, the concubine of King You of Zhou.
  • 申后 - Queen Shen, wife of King You of Zhou.
  • 太子宜臼 - Crown Prince Yijiu, the legitimate heir of King You of Zhou.
  • 伯服 - Bo Fu, son of Bao Si, made crown prince by King You.
  • 申侯 - Lord Shen, father of Queen Shen.
  • 鄫人 - The people of Zeng, an ancient state.

  • 骊山 - Lishan, a mountain near the Zhou capital.
  • 宾媚人 - A historical figure from the *Zuo Zhuan*, known for his role in a state nearly destroyed by his laughter.
  • 齐顷 - Prince Qi Qing, a historical figure from the state of Qi, known for his virtue and filial piety.

  • 外有良医，将作鬼神之事 - A suggestion made by Yanzi to Duke Jing. It must be read as a single suggestion.

  • 棺入殓死者 - Describes Duke Jing's actions in response to Yanzi's suggestion. It must be read as a single action.

  • 小事之成，不若大事之废 - A statement comparing the success of small things to the failure of big things.
  • 君子之非，贤于小人之是也 - A statement about the disapproval of a gentleman being better than the approval of a small person.

  • 虞兵持烛导往溷所 - A soldier named Yu held a candle and led him to the toilet.
  • 经历曲折，殆如永巷 - It was a winding path, almost like a long alley.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 姬 - Refers to a concubine or favored woman, a common practice in feudal China.
  • 專房之愛 - Indicates the concubine's exclusive affection, reflecting the hierarchical nature of relationships in feudal society.
  • 泉下 - A euphemism for death, often used in classical Chinese literature to refer to the underworld.
  • 剛腸膽決 - A metaphor for courage and resolve, emphasizing the contrast between the man's weakness and the general expectation of strength in men.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • His illness was so severe that there was no hope of recovery, a straightforward statement of the man's condition.
  • My heart and courage are only entrusted to you, what shall I do now? This phrase expresses the man's deep emotional dependence on the concubine.
  • The concubine wept and said: 'Sir, please take your medicine. If you were to die, I wish to follow you to the grave.' This shows the concubine's loyalty and willingness to die with her master.
  • Sir, you have killed people like picking off ants and lice, truly a great man. This is a sarcastic remark by a general, criticizing the man's weakness in his final moments.
  • I will wait until he dies first, then I will go. This indicates the concubine's plan to commit suicide after the man's death.
  • He secretly called a strong man to hold a rope and hide behind the bed. This describes the general's plot to kill the concubine.
  • They immediately strangled her neck, and she died shortly after. This describes the execution of the concubine's plan.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 必也两心如结 - Figurative expression meaning 'if two hearts are as closely bound as a knot,' suggesting a deep and unbreakable connection.
  • 计无复之 - Means 'there is no other way to consider' or 'no other option,' indicating a sense of finality.
  • 与其生离，犹冀死合 - Paradoxical phrase meaning 'rather than live apart, one hopes to be united in death,' expressing a preference for death over separation.
  • 幸则为喜、乐 - Means 'if fortunate, it becomes joy and happiness.'
  • 不幸则为傅、林、王、陶 - Refers to tragic figures or fates, possibly alluding to historical or literary examples of misfortune.
  • 死而有知，倡随无愧 - Means 'if death has knowledge, there is no shame in following one's partner,' suggesting a belief in the afterlife and moral integrity.
  • 即令无知，亦省却终身万种凄凉抑郁之苦 - Means 'even if there is no knowledge, it still saves one from a lifetime of countless sorrows and depressions.'
  • 彼痴人者，不自以为得算耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'those foolish people do not consider themselves lucky?'
  • 成帝以之斩嗣 - Refers to Emperor Cheng of Han, who ordered the execution of his son.
  • 幽王以之欺诸侯 - Refers to King You of Zhou, who deceived the feudal lords.
  • 齐、燕二主，以之堕万人之功，弱宗招乱，树敌速亡 - Refers to the rulers of Qi and Yan, who caused the downfall of their states through their actions.
  • 以彼易此，如以千金易一发 - Metaphor meaning 'to exchange one thing for another, like exchanging a thousand gold coins for a single hair,' emphasizing the absurdity of the trade.
  • 虽然，害止此耳 - Means 'although, the harm is limited to this.'
  • 景阳宫之事，岌岌乎兵在其颈，生趣已尽，井中非乐所也 - Refers to the events at Jingyang Palace, where the situation was dire, life had lost its meaning, and the well was not a place of joy.
  • 顽钝无耻，其至矣乎 - Means 'stupid and shameless, this is the extreme.'
  • 夫襚犹先袯，而景公以臭腐为神妙 - Refers to the practice of offering clothes before washing them, and how Duke Jing of Qi considered decayed things as divine.
  • 死欲速朽，而杨政以刀索为衽席 - Means 'to die and wish to decay quickly, but Yang Zheng used a knife and rope as a bed,' suggesting a contrast between desire and reality.
  • 死者生之，而生者死之 - Paradoxical statement meaning 'the dead live, while the living die,' highlighting the reversal of roles.
  • 情之能颠倒人一至于此 - Means 'the power of emotion can reverse people to this extent.'
  • 往以戕人，来以贼己 - Means 'to harm others in the past, to harm oneself in the future.'
  • 小则捐命，大而倾国 - Means 'small actions can lead to the loss of life, and large actions can lead to the downfall of a nation.'
  • 痴人有痴福，惟情不然，何哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'foolish people have foolish luck, but emotions are not like this, why is that?'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '必也两心如结，计无复之' is elliptical, and the connection between the two clauses must be made explicit.
  • The paradoxical nature of the clause '与其生离，犹冀死合' requires careful handling to convey the intended meaning.
  • The conditional structure and the moral implication of '死而有知，倡随无愧' must be clearly expressed.
  • The conditional clause and the contrast with the previous clause of '即令无知，亦省却终身万种凄凉抑郁之苦' must be maintained.
  • The rhetorical question structure of '彼痴人者，不自以为得算耶' must be preserved.
  • The concessive structure and the limitation of harm of '虽然，害止此耳' must be clearly conveyed.
  • The sequence of events and the dire situation of '景阳宫之事，岌岌乎兵在其颈，生趣已尽，井中非乐所也' must be accurately described.
  • The rhetorical question and the emphasis on extreme foolishness of '顽钝无耻，其至矣乎' must be maintained.
  • The contrast between the two clauses of '夫襚犹先袯，而景公以臭腐为神妙' must be clearly expressed.
  • The paradoxical nature of the clause '死欲速朽，而杨政以刀索为衽席' requires careful handling.
  • The reversal of roles of '死者生之，而生者死之' must be clearly conveyed.
  • The emphasis on the power of emotion of '情之能颠倒人一至于此' must be maintained.
  • The contrast between past and future actions of '往以戕人，来以贼己' must be clearly expressed.
  • The scale of consequences of '小则捐命，大而倾国' must be accurately described.
  • The rhetorical question and the contrast with the previous clause of '痴人有痴福，惟情不然，何哉' must be maintained.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 既即位，遂立为后 - After ascending the throne, she was immediately made empress, indicating a swift succession and elevation.
  • 长主挟功怨望，皇后宠遂衰 - The elder princess resented her influence and sought revenge, leading to the empress's declining favor.
  • 骄妒滋甚 - Her arrogance and jealousy grew more severe, describing the worsening of the princess's negative traits.
  • 女巫楚服，自言有术能令上意回 - The female sorceress Chu Fu claimed to have a method to change the emperor's mind, introducing a supernatural element into the narrative.
  • 昼夜祭祀，合药服之 - She performed rituals day and night, mixing and consuming potions, detailing the sorceress's actions.
  • 巫着男子衣冠帻带，与皇后居寝，相爱若夫妇 - The sorceress dressed in men's clothing and lived with the empress, treating her like a husband, highlighting the unusual relationship between the sorceress and the empress.
  • 帝闻，穷治侍御 - The emperor heard and thoroughly investigated the attendants, indicating the emperor's reaction and subsequent actions.
  • 巫与后诸妖蛊咒咀，女而男淫，皆伏辜 - The sorceress and the empress were found guilty of various witchcraft and immoral acts, including lesbian behavior, and were punished, describing the consequences of their actions.
  • 废皇后，处长门宫 - The empress was deposed and confined to the Long Gate Palace, marking a significant change in the empress's status.
  • 后虽废，供养犹如法 - Although deposed, she was still provided for according to the rules, indicating the continued respect for the empress despite her fall from grace.
  • 闻蜀人司马相如有文辞，乃遣人赍千金，求为作《长门赋》 - She heard that the person from Shu, Sima Xiangru, was skilled in writing and sent someone with a thousand gold coins to ask him to write 'The Long Gate Assignment,' introducing a literary element and a request for a work.
  • 叙其哀怨 - To narrate her sorrow and resentment, explaining the purpose of the assignment.
  • 上读之叹息，复迎入宫如初 - The emperor read it and sighed, then welcomed her back into the palace as before, describing the emperor's emotional response and the empress's reinstatement.
  • 以武帝之雄猜，而长门回车，文章信有灵矣 - Given Emperor Wu's suspicious nature, the return of the empress from the Long Gate Palace shows the power of literature indeed, reflecting on the impact of literature.
  • 未几，子夫之立，后安在哉 - Soon after, the Empress Chen was established, where is the empress now, introducing a new empress and raising a question about the fate of the previous one.
  • 于唐之玄宗亦然 - It was also the case with Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, drawing a parallel to another historical figure.
  • 何皇后始以色进，及玄宗即位，不数年恩宠日衰 - Empress He initially gained favor through her beauty, but after Emperor Xuanzong ascended the throne, her favor waned within a few years, describing the decline of Empress He's status.
  • 后忧畏之状，愈不自安 - Her anxiety and fear made her increasingly uneasy, describing her emotional state.
  • 然抚下有恩，幸免谗语共危之祸 - However, she treated her subordinates with kindness and was fortunate enough to avoid the dangers of slander and conspiracy, highlighting her positive qualities.
  • 忽一日泣诉于上曰 - One day, she wept and pleaded with the emperor, saying, introducing a specific incident.
  • 何忠（后父名）脱新紫半臂，更得一斗面，为三郎生日汤饼耶 - My father, He Zhong, once removed his new purple half-sleeve and gave me a measure of flour to make noodles for the Third's birthday, a personal memory shared by the empress.
  • 何忍不追念于前时 - How could you not remember the past, a plea for remembrance.
  • 上恻然改容 - The emperor was moved and changed his expression, describing the emperor's reaction.
  • 由是得延其恩者三年 - As a result, she was able to retain his favor for three more years, indicating the outcome of the empress's plea.
  • 终以武惠妃故，无罪被黜，六宫共怜之 - Ultimately, due to the influence of Empress Wu Hui Fei, she was unjustly deposed, and all the palaces pitied her, concluding the story with a note of injustice and sympathy.

  • 我为学士，尔做夫人 - A playful line in the poem, meaning 'I am a scholar, you are a lady.'
  • 岂不闻陶学士有桃叶、桃根，苏学士有朝云、暮云 - A reference to historical figures, meaning 'Don't you know that Tao the Scholar had Peach Leaf and Peach Root, and Su the Scholar had Morning Cloud and Evening Cloud?' This is a playful allusion to famous scholars and their concubines.
  • 我便多娶几个吴姬越女何过分 - Means 'If I marry several Wu and Yue girls, what is too much?' This is a playful exaggeration of the number of concubines.
  • 你年纪已过四旬，只管占住玉堂春 - Means 'Your age has passed forty, so you should just occupy the spring of the Jade Hall (a metaphor for the spring of life).' This is a gentle rebuke.
  • 你侬我侬，忒煞情多 - Means 'You and I are so affectionate, our love is excessive.' This is a playful and affectionate line.
  • 情多处热如火 - Means 'Where there is so much love, it is as hot as fire.' This is a metaphor for intense passion.
  • 把一块泥，捻一个你，塑一个我 - Means 'Take a piece of clay, mold it into you, mold it into me.' This is a metaphor for creating a perfect match.
  • 将咱两个，一齐打破，用水调和 - Means 'Break both of us, mix us with water.' This is a metaphor for blending together.
  • 再捻一个你，再塑一个我 - Means 'Mold another you, mold another me.' This is a continuation of the metaphor.
  • 我泥中有你，你泥中有我 - Means 'I have you in my clay, you have me in your clay.' This is a metaphor for deep connection.
  • 与你生同一个衾，死同一个椁 - Means 'Live with the same quilt, die in the same coffin.' This is a metaphor for eternal union.
  • 图形诗 - Means 'Poem in the form of a picture,' referring to a poem that describes a visual image.
  • 濠梁人南楚材者 - Means 'A man from Hao and Liang named Nan Chuyan,' where '濠梁' refers to a region in present-day Anhui Province, and '南楚材' is the name of the person.
  • 旅游陈颖 - Means 'Traveling to Chen Ying,' where '陈颖' is a place name.

  • 欲以子妻之 - Means 'Wanted to give his daughter to him as a wife.'
  • 楚材家有妻 - Means 'Nan Chuyan already had a wife.'
  • 重违知己之眷 - Means 'He did not want to go against the affection of his friend,' where '知己' means a close friend.
  • 遂遣家仆妇取琴书，似无返旧之心 - Means 'So he sent his household servants to fetch his musical instruments and books, showing no intention of returning to his old life.'
  • 或谓求道青城，访僧衡岳，不复留心于名宦也 - Means 'Some said he was seeking the Dao in Qingcheng, visiting monks in Hengshan, and no longer cared about official positions.'
  • 其妻薛媛，善书画，好属文 - Means 'His wife Xue Yuan was skilled in painting and calligraphy, and enjoyed writing.'
  • 亦微知其意 - Means 'She also vaguely understood his intentions.'
  • 乃对镜图其形，并诗四韵寄之 - Means 'So she drew his image in front of a mirror and sent a four-line poem to him.'
  • 楚材得妻真及诗，甚惭，遽辞颖牧之命，归而偕老 - Means 'When Nan Chuyan received the portrait and poem, he was deeply ashamed and immediately declined the governor's offer, returning home to live with his wife until old age.'
  • 诗曰 - Means 'The poem says,' introducing the content of the poem.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 湖面征風且莫吹 - Poetic expression meaning 'Do not let the wind on the lake's surface blow.' Uses *征風* ('commanding wind') in a metaphorical or supernatural sense.
  • 浪花初绽月光微 - 'The waves begin to bloom under the faint moonlight.' *浪花* ('wave flowers') is a classical poetic image.
  • 沉潜暗想横波泪 - 'Submerged in deep thought, tears flow like the waves.' *沉潜* ('submerged, deep') and *横波泪* ('tears like waves') are metaphorical and emotional expressions.
  • 得共鲛人相对垂 - 'To weep together with the mermaid.' *鲛人* ('jiao ren') refers to the mythical merfolk of Chinese legend, often associated with weeping and producing pearls.
  • 洞庭风软荻花秋 - 'The wind of Dongting is soft, and the reeds bloom in autumn.' *洞庭* refers to Dongting Lake, a famous location in Chinese literature.
  • 新没青娥细浪愁 - 'Newly submerged is the young maiden in the gentle waves of sorrow.' *青娥* is a poetic term for a young woman or maiden.
  • 泪滴白苹君不见 - 'Tears fall on the white reeds, but you do not see.' *白苹* is a type of water plant, often used in classical poetry.
  • 月明江上有轻鸥 - 'Under the bright moon, the river has a light gull.' A common image in classical poetry of solitude and reflection.
  • 诗成，酹而投之 - 'After the poem was written, he poured wine as an offering and cast it into the water.' *酹* ('pouring wine as an offering') is a ritual gesture in classical Chinese poetry.
  • 精贯神衹 - 'His spirit penetrated the gods.' *神衹* refers to deities or spirits.
  • 遂感水神，持诣水府 - 'He thus moved the water deity, who took him to the water palace.' *水府* is the mythical underwater realm of the water spirits.
  • 府君览之，召溺者数辈曰 - 'The Lord of the Water Palace read it and summoned several drowned people.' *府君* is a title for a local deity or ruler of a realm.
  • 谁是郑生所爱？ - 'Who is the one loved by Zheng Sheng?' A rhetorical question to determine the identity of the beloved.
  • 韦氏亦不能晓其来由 - 'Wei Shi could not understand the reason.' *来由* means 'reason' or 'cause.'
  • 主者搜臂见红绡 - 'The attendant searched her arm and found the red silk.' *主者* is the attendant or official in charge.
  • 德璘异日是吾邑之明宰 - 'De Lian will one day be the bright governor of my town.' *明宰* is a respectful term for a wise and capable official.
  • 况曩日有义相及，不可不曲活尔命 - 'Moreover, in the past you had kindness toward me; I cannot fail to spare your life.' *曩日* means 'in the past' or 'formerly.'
  • 遂纳为室，感其异也，将归长沙 - 'He then took her as his wife, moved by her extraordinary nature, and returned her to Changsha.' *纳为室* is a formal way of saying 'took as a wife.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '女遂于襦带解蹙金结花合子，又抽翠玉双凤翘一只赠季衡' describes a woman unfastening a golden brocade pouch and removing a pair of green jade phoenix hairpins to give to Ji Heng. The sequence of actions is clear but requires careful rendering to maintain the flow.
  • The phrase '望异日睹物思人，无以幽冥为隔' expresses a wish to see the object and think of the person, with no separation between the living and the dead. The structure is straightforward but carries deep emotional weight.
  • The sentence '此物虽非珍异，但贵其名如意，愿长在玉手操持耳' explains that the object is not particularly valuable but is cherished for its name and the hope that it will always be held by the beloved's hands. The concessive structure ('虽非...但贵其...') requires careful translation.
  • The phrase '非一甲子，无相见期' means 'not for sixty years will we meet again,' emphasizing the long separation. The structure is clear but requires a precise rendering to convey the temporal aspect.
  • The phrase '言讫呜咽而没' describes a soft, sobbing cry followed by disappearance, conveying a sense of sorrow and finality. The brevity of the sentence requires careful handling to maintain the emotional impact.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 趙鳏居 - Zhao was a widower. In Classical Chinese, *鳏居* means to be a widower (a man whose wife has died) and to live alone.
  • 見詩，遂求媒焉 - Upon seeing a poem, he then sought a matchmaker. *求媒* is the act of seeking a matchmaker for marriage. *焉* is a particle used to mark the end of the clause.
  • 人以為二十八字媒云 - People called it a matchmaker of twenty-eight characters. *二十八字* refers to the number of characters in the poem, *媒* is 'matchmaker' or 'agent of marriage,' and *云* is a classical particle meaning 'say' or 'it is said that.'
  • 清江引 - A title of a *qu* (Chinese opera song form), also known as *qinjiang yin* or *Qingjiang Yin*, which is a specific musical form in Chinese zaju (variety drama).
  • 樂人 - Musician or performer. In the context of *李四之妻* (wife of Li Si), it likely means a professional musician or performer.
  • 江右人 - A person from Jiangyou. *江右* is a classical term for the area west of the Yangtze River, which corresponds roughly to modern-day Jiangxi Province.
  • 與楊春秀同時 - She was contemporary with Yang Chunxiu. *同時* means 'contemporary' or 'at the same time.'
  • 頗通文墨 - She was quite well-versed in writing and calligraphy. *文墨* refers to literary and written skills.
  • 滑稽、歌舞迥出其流 - Her wit and performances were far above her peers. *滑稽* is 'humorous' or 'jovial,' *歌舞* is 'singing and dancing,' and *迥出其流* means 'far surpassing her contemporaries.'
  • 時貴多重之 - At that time, the nobles and officials greatly valued her. *貴人* refers to the elite or high-ranking individuals, and *重之* is 'valued her.'
  • 先與撫州常推官之子三舍者交好 - She had previously been in a relationship with the son of the magistrate of Fuzhou, named Sanshe. *推官* is a magistrate or local official, and *三舍* is a name or possibly a title.
  • 苦其夫間阻 - She was distressed by her husband's interference. *間阻* means 'to interfere' or 'to obstruct.'
  • 一日偕宵遁 - One day, she fled at night with him. *宵遁* is a classical term for fleeing at night.
  • 事覺，決杖 - The matter was discovered, and she was sentenced to a beating. *決杖* is a punishment involving the beating with a stick.
  • 劉負愧，將之廣海居焉 - Liu, burdened with guilt, was going to live in Guanghai. *負愧* means 'to feel ashamed' or 'to carry guilt,' and *廣海* is a place name, possibly a coastal or distant region.
  • 道經赣州 - On the way, she passed through Ganzhou. *道經* is 'passed through' or 'traveled through.'
  • 全音庵撥里，字子仁 - Quan Yinan Bolì, courtesy name Ziren. *撥里* is likely a surname or a title, and *字* is the courtesy name.
  • 由禮部尚書值天下多故，選用除赣州監郡 - Because of the many troubles in the world, he was selected from the position of Minister of Rites to serve as the supervisor of Ganzhou. *值天下多故* means 'during a time of many troubles,' *選用除* is 'was selected and appointed to,' and *監郡* is a supervisory official over a region.
  • 平昔文章政事揚歷台省 - In the past, his writings and governance had been prominent in the central government. *台省* refers to the central bureaucratic institutions.
  • 但未免花酒之癖 - But he could not avoid the habit of drinking and courting women. *花酒* is a classical term for drinking and flirting with women.
  • 每日公餘，即與士夫酣歌賦詩 - Every day after official duties, he would sing and compose poetry with the literati. *士夫* refers to the educated class or officials.
  • 帽上常喜簪花，否則或果或葉亦簪一枝 - He often liked to wear flowers in his hat; otherwise, he would wear a branch of fruit or leaves. *簪花* is to insert flowers into one’s hair or hat.
  • 劉之廣海過赣，謁全公 - Liu, on her way to Guanghai, passed through Ganzhou and paid homage to Quan Gong. *謁* is to visit or pay respects to someone of higher rank.
  • 妾欲之廣海，誓不復還 - I wish to go to Guanghai, and I swear I will not return. *妾* is a humble term for a woman.
  • 久聞尚書清譽，獲一見而逝，死無恨也 - I have long heard of the esteemed reputation of the Minister of Rites. If I may see him once and then pass away, I shall have no regrets. *清譽* is 'pure reputation' or 'esteemed reputation.'
  • 全哀其志而與進焉 - Quan pitied her resolve and allowed her to enter. *哀其志* is 'pitied her determination.'
  • 時賓朋滿座 - At that time, the hall was full of guests. *賓朋* are guests and friends.
  • 全帽上簪青梅一枝 - Quan had a branch of green plum in his hat. *青梅* is unripe green plum, a symbol of youth or modesty.
  • 行酒，全口占《清江引》曲云 - As he passed around the wine, Quan composed a *Qingjiang Yin* song on the spot. *口占* is to compose and recite a poem or song on the spot.
  • 青青子兒枝上結 - Green, green, the little fruit grows on the branch. *子兒* is a colloquial term for 'fruit' or 'seed,' and *枝上結* is 'grows on the branch.'
  • 令賓朋續之 - He ordered the guests to continue it. *續之* is 'to continue it.'
  • 眾亡有對者 - None of the guests could respond. *亡* is a variant of *無*, meaning 'none.'
  • 劉應聲曰 - Liu responded immediately.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 元方所遇，義虎也 - Metaphorical expression where '義虎' is a tiger of righteousness or virtue.
  • 自励所遇，忠虎也 - '自励' implies self-discipline or self-motivation, and '忠虎' is a metaphor for a loyal or faithful tiger.
  • 义兴陈氏所遇，媚虎也 - '媚虎' is a metaphor for a tiger that is flattering or seductive, indicating a person who is insincere or manipulative.
  • 总之无愧于虎媒也 - '虎媒' is a metaphor for a tiger matchmaker, implying a matchmaker who is as strong or noble as a tiger. '无愧' means 'without shame' or 'not lacking in honor.'
  • 撮合为媒 - Straightforward phrase meaning 'to bring together as a matchmaker.'
  • 越客婚有日矣 - '婚有日矣' means 'the wedding has a set date.'
  • 虎一番惊扰，大为嘉礼之累 - '嘉礼' is a classical term for a grand or auspicious ceremony, often referring to a wedding. The phrase means 'the tiger’s disturbance is a great hindrance to the auspicious ceremony.'
  • 板屋声高，馋口未厌 - '板屋' may refer to a simple or rustic house. '馋口未厌' means 'the greedy mouth is not yet satisfied,' suggesting a person who is insatiable or greedy.
  • 忠者见杀，恶者居功 - A moral contrast: the loyal are killed, while the wicked take credit. This is a common classical expression of injustice.
  • 此为媒者之所以竞为恶，而莫肯尽忠也 - Complex sentence meaning 'this is why matchmakers compete to be wicked, and none are willing to be entirely loyal.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大别狐 - Appears to be the title or name of the author or the source of the text. '狐' means 'fox,' which in classical Chinese literature often symbolizes a trickster or a supernatural being.
  • 天顺甲申年间 - '天顺' is the era name of the Ming emperor Tianshun (reigned 1457–1464), and '甲申' is a Chinese sexagenary year corresponding to the year 1454. This may be a mistake, as '天顺' began in 1457.
  • 浙中蒋生 - '浙中' refers to the central region of Zhejiang province. '蒋生' is a proper noun, likely the name of a character or a scholar.
  • 汉阳马口某店 - '汉阳' is a city in Hubei province. '马口' may be a specific location or a surname. '某店' means 'a certain shop' or 'a particular store.'
  • 临窗纤姣，光采射人 - '纤姣' means 'delicate and beautiful,' and '光采射人' is a poetic expression meaning 'radiant beauty that dazzles the eyes.'
  • 窃见之，叹羡魂销 - '窃见' is a modest expression meaning 'I secretly saw.' '叹羡魂销' means 'to be so moved by admiration that one’s soul is consumed,' a poetic way of expressing deep love or infatuation.
  • 家严刚厉 - '家严' is a respectful term for one’s father. '刚厉' means 'stern' or 'severe.'
  • 慎口修持 - Classical phrase meaning 'to be careful with one’s words and maintain self-discipline.'
  • 齿尚少，美丰仪 - '齿尚少' means 'still young,' and '美丰仪' means 'handsome and well-proportioned,' referring to a young man’s good appearance.
  • 口必三缄，足不外趾 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to keep one’s mouth tightly closed (not speak)' and 'not to step outside (remain faithful).'
  • 惟恐负女 - '惟恐' means 'only fearing' or 'only afraid of.' '负女' means 'disappointing the woman.'
  • 渐惫瘁 - '惫' means 'exhausted,' and '瘁' means 'worn out' or 'in poor health.' Together, it suggests the man is becoming increasingly weak or ill.
  • 侪若夜闻人声，疑之 - '侪' refers to one’s peers or companions. '夜闻人声' means 'heard voices at night.' '疑之' means 'suspected it.'
  • 君得无中妖乎 - A question meaning 'Are you not involved with a demon or a spirit?' '中妖' is a common expression for being influenced by a supernatural being.
  • 讳匿 - To conceal or hide something.
  • 疾力 - Term that can mean 'with great effort' or 'with all one’s might.'
  • 与马公女有前缘，常自来欢会 - '前缘' is a Buddhist term meaning 'karmic connection' or 'fateful meeting.' '欢会' means 'to meet in secret for romantic purposes.'

  • 此无不可，第以法禁甚严，欲置老夫罪戾也 - Means 'there is nothing wrong with this, but the laws are very strict, and it would be a crime to place me in danger,' indicating the uncle's concern about legal consequences.
  • 媒知其不就，因告归 - Means 'the matchmaker knew he would not agree, so he reported back,' indicating the matchmaker's decision.
  • 舅又命妗酌酒与媒为别 - Means 'the uncle then instructed the aunt to serve wine to the matchmaker as a farewell,' indicating a formal goodbye.
  • 娇因侍立 - Means 'the young lady stood respectfully by,' showing her presence during the farewell.
  • 私语媒曰 - Means 'she then privately spoke to the matchmaker,' indicating a confidential conversation.
  • 离合缘契，乃天为之也 - Means 'the separation and reunion are determined by fate,' indicating a belief in destiny.
  • 三兄无事宜来 - Means 'my brother should come if there is any matter,' indicating a suggestion for her brother to intervene.
  • 妾年且长，岁月有限 - Means 'I am getting older, and time is limited,' indicating her awareness of her age.
  • 无以姻事不谐为念 - Means 'do not worry about the failure of our marriage,' indicating her reassurance to her brother.
  • 因出手书，令媒持归，以复于生 - Means 'she then handed over the letter, instructing the matchmaker to take it back to life,' indicating the final step in the process.
  • 媒既归，道舅不允之由 - Means 'the matchmaker returned and explained why the uncle did not agree,' indicating the matchmaker's report.
  • 遂以娇书与生 - Means 'he then gave the young lady's letter to life,' indicating the final delivery of the letter.
  • 生展视，乃新词《满庭芳》一阕也 - Means 'life opened it and found it was a new poem titled 'Man Ting Fang,' indicating the content of the letter.

  • 厚卿 - Likely refers to a man named Hou Qing, possibly a suitor or a close friend of the young lady.
  • 舅 - Refers to an uncle, specifically a maternal uncle in this context.
  • 《满庭芳》 - A classical Chinese poem form, often used for expressing emotions and thoughts.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 成都府角妓丁怜怜 - Refers to a courtesan named Ding Lianni in Chengdu. Courtesans were educated and skilled women who entertained the elite.
  • 帅府 - Refers to the military governor's office, indicating a high-ranking official in the military hierarchy.
  • 眉州王通判 - Refers to the newly appointed magistrate of Meizhou. '通判' was a local government official in charge of administrative and judicial duties.
  • 西施妃子 - Refers to Xi Shi and the concubines, legendary beauties from ancient China, often used as a standard of beauty.
  • 帅府幼子 - Refers to the young son of the military governor, indicating a member of the elite class.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 常捐数千缗 - Frequently donated several thousand qian (a unit of currency). 缗 refers to a string of coins, typically 1,000 coins, so 数千缗 is a large sum of money.
  • 伺隙绘人家美女以献 - Waited for an opportunity to paint beautiful women from households and present them. 伺隙 means to wait for an opportunity or gap, implying secrecy or stealth.
  • 色莹肌白 - Her skin was radiant and white. This is a classical way of describing beauty, often used in poetry and fiction.
  • 眼长而媚 - Her eyes were long and alluring. A common classical description of beauty.
  • 爱作合蝉鬓 - She loved to style her hair in a hechan (compound cicada) bun. 合蝉鬓 is a specific hairstyle, often associated with elegance and beauty in classical literature.
  • 时有忧怨不足之状 - She often showed a look of sorrow and dissatisfaction. This suggests an emotional or melancholic expression.
  • 常至帅府内室见之 - He often visited the inner chambers of the general's mansion to see her. 帅府 refers to the residence of a military general.
  • 因记其姓字，果是否？ - He remembered her name and surname, is that correct? This is a rhetorical question used to confirm identity.
  • 妾每见其图，佇目不能去 - Every time I see her portrait, I cannot take my eyes off it. 佇目 means to gaze fixedly or longingly.
  • 第恨不睹其人 - Only regretting that I have not seen her in person. 第 here means 'only' or 'just.'
  • 丐我 - Ask me for it or beg me for it. Used in the context of requesting something.
  • 生诺之 - The young man agreed to it. 诺之 is a formal way of agreeing or promising.
  • 伤感成疾，困卧累日 - He became ill from sorrow and lay in bed for several days. 伤感成疾 is a classical way of expressing emotional distress leading to illness.
  • 托以梦寐绝怪将不能免 - He claimed that strange dreams and omens could not be avoided. 梦寐绝怪 refers to strange dreams or supernatural phenomena.
  • 求善能驱役鬼神者作法禳之 - He sought someone skilled in commanding ghosts and spirits to perform a ritual to ward it off. 驱役鬼神 is a term from Taoist and folk religious practices.
  • 令向父母言，此为鬼物所凭，必当远避，方可向安 - Instructed the witch to tell the parents that this is a possession by a spirit, and that it must be avoided to ensure safety. 鬼物所凭 means 'possessed by a ghostly spirit.'
  • 生死未判 - The fate of life and death is uncertain. A common phrase in classical Chinese to express uncertainty about survival.
  • 舅家 - The residence of the maternal uncle or the aunt's house. 舅 refers to the maternal uncle, and 家 is a common suffix for 'house' or 'residence.'
  • 娇时在父母旁，闻生有来期，喜慰特甚 - Jiao was by her parents' side, and upon hearing of the young man's arrival, she was especially joyful. 娇 is likely a name or a term of endearment.
  • 生亦随觉病差 - The young man also felt his illness improve. 病差 means 'improved in health' or 'recovered.'

  • 父母在，焉可远游 - Formal and moral expression meaning 'with parents present, how can we travel far?' '焉可' (yān kě) means 'how can' or 'how could,' and '远游' (yuǎn yóu) means 'travel far.'
  • 然舅命难违，弟固当往 - Means 'but the uncle's command is hard to disobey; I should go.' '难违' (nán wéi) means 'hard to disobey,' and '固当往' (gù dāng wǎng) means 'should go.'
  • 于是生欣然治行，诣舅住所 - Means 'thus, the son happily prepared to travel and went to the uncle's residence.' '治行' (zhì xíng) means 'to prepare to travel,' and '诣' (yì) means 'to go to' or 'to visit.'
  • 既至，舅见之，且贺且谢 - Means 'upon arrival, the uncle saw him and both congratulated and thanked him.' '且贺且谢' (qiě hè qiě xiè) means 'both congratulated and thanked.'
  • 须臾，妗、娇毕见 - Means 'soon, the aunt and the daughter-in-law were also seen.' '须臾' (xū yú) means 'soon' or 'in a short while,' and '毕见' (bì jiàn) means 'all appeared.'

  • 妗终以生前疑似之故，馆生于厅事之东边，去堂甚远 - Means 'the aunt, due to the suspicion that existed before, housed the son in a room in the east wing of the hall, far from the main hall.' '馆生' (guǎn shēng) means 'housed the son,' and '去堂甚远' (qù táng shèn yuǎn) means 'far from the main hall.'
  • 生亦远嫌，寻常非呼召不入 - Means 'the son also avoided suspicion and would not enter unless called.' '远嫌' (yuǎn xián) means 'avoid suspicion,' and '非呼召不入' (fēi hū zhào bù rù) means 'would not enter unless called.'
  • 纵或一至堂庑，未尝与娇款狎 - Means 'even if he went to the hall or the courtyard, he never engaged in intimate conversation with the daughter-in-law.' '纵或' (zòng huò) means 'even if,' and '未尝' (wèi cháng) means 'never.'
  • 或与娇偶然相遇，左右森立，但彼此佇视，不能出一言 - Means 'or if he met the daughter-in-law by chance, there were people standing around, and they just stood there looking at each other, unable to say a word.' '左右森立' (zuǒ yòu sēn lì) means 'people standing around,' and '但彼此佇视' (dàn bǐ cǐ zhù shì) means 'just stood there looking at each other.'
  • 生殊无聊，住十余日，欲告归 - Means 'the son felt very bored, stayed for more than ten days, and wanted to return home.' '殊无聊' (shū wú liáo) means 'very bored,' and '欲告归' (yù gào guī) means 'wanted to return home.'
  • 然终念远来未曾与娇一语，闷闷不适，徘徊久之 - Means 'but he still thought that he had come from afar and had never spoken to the daughter-in-law, feeling depressed and uncomfortable, and wandered for a long time.' '终念' (zhōng niàn) means 'still thought,' '远来' (yuǎn lái) means 'came from afar,' '未曾与娇一语' (wèi céng yǔ jiāo yī yǔ) means 'had never spoken to the daughter-in-law,' '闷闷不适' (mèn mèn bù shì) means 'feeling depressed and uncomfortable,' and '徘徊久之' (pái huái jiǔ zhī) means 'wandered for a long time.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 遂遣人致书，且询问'二甥荣授何官，如瓜期未及，能一来款我，以慰老夫忻喜之心否？' - This sentence is a complex structure that combines the act of sending a letter with a series of questions. The questions are embedded within the main clause and must be read together to understand the full meaning.
  • 生得书，与兄谋曰 - This sentence introduces the son's reaction to receiving the letter and his consultation with his brother. The structure is clear but requires careful parsing to maintain the flow of the narrative.
  • 舅、妗问劳尽礼 - This sentence describes the formal and respectful manner in which the uncle and aunt treated the son. The structure is straightforward but emphasizes the importance of courtesy.
  • 妗终以生前疑似之故，馆生于厅事之东边，去堂甚远 - This sentence explains the aunt's decision to house the son in a distant room due to past suspicions. The structure is complex and requires careful translation to convey the reason and the spatial relationship.
  • 或与娇偶然相遇，左右森立，但彼此佇视，不能出一言 - This sentence describes a scene where the son and the daughter-in-law meet by chance, with people standing around, and they just look at each other without speaking. The structure is descriptive and requires careful rendering to capture the atmosphere.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • Ambiguity in '君近日何相弃': Can be interpreted as 'Why have you been neglecting me lately?' or 'Why have you been treating me so coldly lately?'
  • Ambiguity in '妾之待兄亦至矣': Can be interpreted as 'I have been waiting for you with utmost patience' or 'I have been waiting for you with great effort.'
  • Ambiguity in '一旦若是': Can be interpreted as 'Suddenly, if it were so' or 'Suddenly, if it were like this.'
  • Ambiguity in '岂平昔所望于兄者': Can be interpreted as 'Is this what I have always hoped for from you?' or 'Is this what I have always expected from you?'
  • Ambiguity in '兄每夕所遇者何人': Can be interpreted as 'Who do you meet every night?' or 'Who do you encounter every night?'
  • Ambiguity in '左右顾盼': Can be interpreted as 'Looking around to see if anyone is listening' or 'Looking around to check if anyone is watching.'
  • Ambiguity in '切切': Can be interpreted as 'urgently' or 'earnestly.'
  • Ambiguity in '子令我勿言': Can be interpreted as 'You told me not to speak' or 'You made me keep silent.'
  • Ambiguity in '何窘我也': Can be interpreted as 'Why are you making me feel uncomfortable?' or 'Why are you putting me in an awkward position?'
  • Ambiguity in '妾有何事，令君勿言': Can be interpreted as 'What have I done to make you keep silent?' or 'What have I done to make you not speak?'
  • Ambiguity in '左右有人乎': Can be interpreted as 'Are there people around?' or 'Is anyone nearby?'
  • Ambiguity in '自别君之后': Can be interpreted as 'Since I parted from you' or 'Since we separated.'
  • Ambiguity in '迄今将两岁矣': Can be interpreted as 'It has been nearly two years' or 'It has been almost two years.'
  • Ambiguity in '妾亦何便得与君款密': Can be interpreted as 'How could I have had the chance to be close to you?' or 'How could I have had the opportunity to be intimate with you?'
  • Ambiguity in '何尝嘱君勿言': Can be interpreted as 'I have never told you not to speak' or 'I have never instructed you to keep silent.'
  • Ambiguity in '子何反复也': Can be interpreted as 'Why are you being inconsistent?' or 'Why are you changing your mind?'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 恨，含叹何言 - Expresses deep sorrow and frustration, literally 'hate, what can be said in sighs?'
  • 倘仁人用心，俯遂幽劣 - A conditional phrase meaning 'if a benevolent person uses their heart, they will humbly follow the lowly,' indicating a plea for compassion.
  • 虽死之日，犹生之年 - A hyperbolic expression meaning 'even on the day of death, it is like a year of life,' emphasizing the depth of feeling.
  • 如或达士略情，舍小从大 - Means 'if a wise person overlooks feelings, they will abandon the small for the great,' suggesting a rational decision-making process.
  • 以先配为丑行，谓要盟之可欺 - A conditional phrase meaning 'if one considers the former match as a shameful act, believing that a solemn promise can be deceived,' indicating a moral dilemma.
  • 因风委露，犹托清尘 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to entrust oneself to the wind and dew, still relying on pure dust,' suggesting a desire for purity and remembrance.
  • 存没之诚，言尽于此 - Means 'the sincerity of life and death, all said here,' indicating the completeness of the message.
  • 临纸呜咽，情不能申 - Describes the emotional state of writing, 'at the paper, sobbing, unable to express fully,' conveying deep emotion.
  • 千万珍重，珍重千万 - A repeated phrase meaning 'a thousand times cherish, cherish a thousand times,' emphasizing the importance of care.
  • 玉环一枚，是儿婴年所弄，寄充君子下体所佩 - Describes a jade ring, 'a jade ring, one piece, which the child played with in infancy, entrusted to adorn the gentleman's lower body,' indicating a personal and sentimental gift.
  • 玉其坚润不渝，环取其终始不绝 - Describes the qualities of the jade and the ring, 'the jade is firm and lustrous, never changing; the ring takes its beginning and end without interruption,' emphasizing the enduring nature of the gift.
  • 因物达诚，永以为好耳 - Means 'through objects to convey sincerity, forever to be good,' indicating the lasting nature of the relationship.
  • 心迩身遐，拜会无期 - Describes the emotional and physical distance, 'the heart is close, but the body is far, no meeting in sight,' expressing longing.
  • 幽愤所钟，千里神合 - Means 'the deep resentment is concentrated, spirits unite over a thousand miles,' indicating a strong emotional connection despite physical separation.
  • 春风多厉，强饭为佳 - Describes the harshness of spring winds, 'spring winds are often harsh, eat well,' indicating the need for self-care.
  • 慎言自保，无以鄙为深念 - Offers further advice, 'speak carefully, protect yourself, do not let me be deeply concerned,' providing additional emotional support.
  • 千万珍重！ - A repeated phrase meaning 'a thousand times cherish,' emphasizing the importance of care.

  • 乱丝一絇 - Refers to a tangle of silk, symbolizing confusion or entanglement.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 微月透帘栊 - A faint moonlight filters through the lattice windows. *微月* is a poetic way to describe a dim or waning moon, and *帘栊* refers to the lattice windows of a traditional Chinese room, often used in poetry to evoke a quiet, intimate setting.
  • 萤光度碧空 - Firefly lights drift through the azure sky. *萤光* literally means 'firefly light,' but is used poetically here to describe a gentle, luminous glow. *碧空* is a classical term for the clear blue sky.
  • 遥天初缥缈 - The distant sky is just beginning to appear ethereal. *缥缈* is a poetic term for something faint, distant, or dreamlike.
  • 低树渐葱茏 - Low trees gradually become lush and green. *葱茏* is a poetic way to describe verdant, flourishing vegetation.
  • 龙吹过庭竹 - The dragon's breath passes through the courtyard bamboo. *龙吹* is a metaphorical or mythical expression, likely referring to a powerful wind or sound, possibly from a dragon, a common motif in classical Chinese poetry.
  • 鸾歌拂井桐 - The phoenix's song brushes the well-side paulownia. *鸾歌* is the song of the *luan*, a mythical bird often associated with the phoenix, and *井桐* refers to the *paulownia* tree growing by a well, a traditional poetic image.
  • 罗绡垂薄露 - Silken gauze hangs with dew. *罗绡* is a type of fine silk or gauze fabric, often used to describe veils or draperies in poetry.
  • 环佩响轻风 - The sound of jade rings echoes in the gentle breeze. *环佩* refers to the jade ornaments worn by women, which clink together when they move.
  • 绛节随金母 - The crimson staff follows the Queen Mother of Heaven. *绛节* is a red staff or scepter, and *金母* is the Queen Mother of Heaven (*Jinmu*), a deity in Daoist mythology.
  • 云心捧玉童 - The cloud-hearted attendants carry the jade boys. *云心* is a poetic way to describe cloud-like hearts or a pure, ethereal spirit, and *玉童* is a term for jade-clad attendants or young boys in Daoist imagery.
  • 更深人悄悄 - Deeper into the night, people are quiet. *更深* refers to the later hours of the night.
  • 晨会雨濛濛 - At dawn, the rain falls gently. *濛濛* is a poetic term for light, continuous rain.
  • 珠润光文履 - The pearls shine on the embroidered shoes. *文履* refers to ornate or embroidered shoes, often worn by women.
  • 花明隐绣笼 - The flowers glow, hidden within the embroidered cage. *绣笼* is a term for an embroidered cage or enclosure, possibly a metaphor for a chamber or a decorative setting.
  • 瑶钗行彩凤 - The jade hairpin moves like a colorful phoenix. *瑶钗* is a jade hairpin, and *彩凤* is a colorful phoenix, a common poetic image.
  • 罗帔掩丹虹 - The silk robe hides the crimson rainbow. *罗帔* is a silk robe or shawl, and *丹虹* is a red rainbow, often used in poetic imagery to describe a woman’s beauty.
  • 言自瑶华浦 - She speaks from the Jade Flower Marsh. *瑶华浦* is a poetic place name, likely a metaphorical or mythical location.
  • 将朝碧玉宫 - She is about to go to the Green Jade Palace. *碧玉宫* is a Daoist or mythological palace, often associated with the Queen Mother of Heaven.
  • 因游里（洛）城北 - Because she was wandering in the northern part of the city (Luoyang). *里* is a measure word for a district or neighborhood, and the text seems to have a missing character, likely *洛* (Luoyang), a major city in ancient China.
  • 偶向宋家东 - She happened to go east of the Song family’s residence. *宋家* likely refers to a noble or aristocratic family, possibly the *Song* family of the Song Dynasty, though the exact reference is uncertain.
  • 戏调初微拒 - At first, she gently resists playful teasing. *戏调* means playful banter or flirtation.
  • 柔情已暗通 - Her tender feelings have already been secretly shared. *暗通* implies a secret or hidden emotional connection.
  • 低环蝉影动 - Her low-hanging hair ring stirs with the cicada’s shadow. *低环* may refer to a hair ornament or a low ring on her hair, and *蝉影* is the shadow of a cicada, symbolizing the summer season.
  • 回步玉尘蒙 - She turns her steps, cloaked in the dust of jade. *玉尘* is a poetic term for fine dust or powder, often associated with beauty or purity.
  • 转面流花雪 - She turns her face, like flowing snow and flowers. *流花雪* is a poetic image of beauty, combining the imagery of falling flowers and snow.
  • 登床抱绮丛 - She climbs onto the bed and embraces the brocade cluster. *绮丛* may refer to a cluster of brocade or embroidered items, or metaphorically to a group of people or a romantic setting.
  • 鸳鸯交颈舞 - The mandarin ducks dance with their necks entwined. *鸳鸯* is a symbol of love and marriage in Chinese culture.
  • 翡翠合欢笼 - The emerald cage of joyous union. *翡翠* is jade or emerald, and *合欢* is a term for conjugal love or a type of plant symbolizing union.
  • 眉黛羞偏聚 - Her eyebrows, dark as ink, blush and gather. *眉黛* is a poetic way to describe dark, elegant eyebrows, often compared to ink or jade.
  • 唇朱暖更融 - Her red lips warm and soften. *唇朱* refers to red lips, and *暖更融* suggests a soft, melting warmth, possibly implying a romantic or intimate setting.
  • 气清兰蕊馥 - Her breath is clear, like the fragrance of orchid petals. *兰蕊* is the delicate petals of the orchid, a symbol of purity and elegance.
  • 肤润玉肌丰 - Her skin is smooth and plump as jade. *玉肌* is a poetic way to describe smooth, white skin.
  • 无力慵移履（腕） - She is too weak to move her foot (or wrist). *履* is a shoe, but the term *腕* (wrist) may be an alternative or a scribal error. The phrase suggests a moment of physical or emotional weakness.
  • 汗光珠点点 - Dewy beads of sweat glisten. *汗光* is the gleam of sweat, and *珠点点* is a poetic way to describe it as scattered pearls.
  • 发乱绿葱葱 - Her hair is disheveled, green and lush. *葱葱* is a poetic term for lush or vibrant green, possibly describing her hair as bright and healthy.
  • 方喜千年会 - Just as she is happy to meet after a thousand years. *千年会* is a poetic expression of a long-awaited reunion.
  • 俄闻五夜穷 - Suddenly, she hears the end of the fifth night. *五夜* may refer to the five nights of a festival or a period of time, and *穷* means the end or exhaustion of that time.
  • 流连时有限 - The time for lingering is limited. *流连* means to linger or stay, and *有限* is the limitation of time.
  • 缱绻意难终 - The tender affection is hard to end. *缱绻* is a term for deep, affectionate feelings.
  • 慢脸含愁态 - Her slow face holds a sorrowful expression. *慢脸* may be a poetic way to describe a face that is slow to express emotion, or it may be a typo for *漫脸* (a full face).
  • 芳词誓素衷 - Her fragrant words swear to a pure heart. *芳词* is poetic language, and *素衷* is a pure or sincere heart.
  • 赠环明运合 - She gives a ring, a sign of a bright fate. *赠环* is a traditional gesture of giving a ring as a token of love or fate, and *明运合* suggests a harmonious or auspicious destiny.
  • 留结表心同 - She ties the knot, showing that their hearts are one. *留结* is a metaphor for tying a knot, often associated with marriage or binding love.
  • 啼粉流清镜 - Her tears flow like powder over the clear mirror. *啼粉* is a poetic way to describe tears.

Proper Nouns

  • 金母 - The Queen Mother of Heaven (*Jinmu*), a deity in Daoist mythology.
  • 洛 - Luoyang, a major city in ancient China.
  • 宋家 - A noble or aristocratic family, possibly the *Song* family of the Song Dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 誠难度，天高不易冲 - Poetic expression suggesting that true difficulty lies in reaching the heavens, symbolizing the challenges of achieving greatness.
  • 行云无处所，箫史在楼中 - Metaphorical expression where '行云无处所' means 'clouds wander without a fixed place,' and '箫史在楼中' refers to the legendary figure Xiang Shi (萧史), who was said to play the flute so beautifully that he could ascend to heaven.
  • 大凡天之所命尤物也 - Means 'generally speaking, those who are especially favored by heaven.'
  • 不妖其身，必妖于人 - Metaphorical expression suggesting that if someone is not beautiful or extraordinary themselves, they will cause trouble for others.
  • 乘宠娇 - Means 'to take advantage of one's beauty or charm.'
  • 为云为雨 - Metaphorically means 'to become a cloud or rain,' suggesting transformation or change.
  • 为蛟为螭 - Metaphorically means 'to become a dragon or a mythical creature,' symbolizing powerful and unpredictable transformations.
  • 弃置今何道 - Means 'what is the way to abandon now.'
  • 当时且自亲 - Means 'at that time, I was still close to you.'
  • 还将旧来意 - Means 'I still hold the old feelings.'
  • 怜取眼前人 - Means 'cherish the person in front of you.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 殷之辛 - Refers to King Zhou of Shang, the last ruler of the Shang dynasty, known for his tyranny and the fall of the dynasty.
  • 周之幽 - Refers to King You of Zhou, the last ruler of the Western Zhou dynasty, known for his misrule and the eventual collapse of the dynasty.
  • 妖孽 - In this context, it refers to evil or harmful influences, often associated with supernatural or moral corruption.

Proper Nouns

  • 殷之辛 - King Zhou of Shang.
  • 周之幽 - King You of Zhou.
  • 崔氏子 - Cui's daughter.
  • 张生 - Zhang's son.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 自是，绝不复知矣 - Classical expression meaning 'from then on, it was no longer known,' indicating a loss of knowledge or memory.
  • 善补过者 - Literally 'good at correcting mistakes,' but in context, it refers to someone who is adept at making amends or rectifying faults.
  • 负心薄幸、食言背盟之徒 - Refers to people who are unfaithful, unkind, and break their promises, often used in the context of romantic relationships.
  • 女子钟情之深，无如崔者 - Means 'no woman is as deeply devoted as Cui,' highlighting Cui's intense emotional attachment.
  • 乱而终之，犹可救过之半 - Means 'even if it ends in chaos, half of the fault can still be corrected,' suggesting that some redemption is possible despite the outcome.
  • 妖不自我，何畏乎尤物 - Philosophical statement meaning 'the disaster is not caused by me, so why should I fear the extraordinary,' implying a lack of personal responsibility.
  • 微之与李十郎一也 - Means 'Wei Zhi and Li Shilang are the same,' suggesting a similarity in their actions or character.
  • 特崔不能为小玉耳 - Means 'only Cui could not be like Xiao Yu,' referring to a famous courtesan known for her beauty and virtue.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 班婕妤 - Title given to a concubine of the Han Dynasty, specifically referring to Ban Jieyu, known for her literary talent and virtue.

  • 圣贤之君，名贤在侧 - Means 'saintly and wise rulers had virtuous people by their side,' highlighting the importance of moral advisors.
  • 三代昏主，乃有嬖妾 - Means 'rulers of the Three Dynasties (Shang, Zhou, and Qin) had favored concubines,' contrasting with the previous statement.
  • 得无似乎 - Means 'would it not be inappropriate?' expressing doubt about the propriety of the emperor's request.
  • 上善其言而止 - Means 'the emperor appreciated her words and stopped,' indicating he accepted her reasoning.
  • 飞燕姊弟用事 - Means 'the Emperor's favorite, Feiyan and her brother, gained power,' referring to Zhao Feiyan and her brother Zhao Yiyang.
  • 谮其咒诅 - Means 'accused her of cursing,' indicating false accusations against Ban Jieyu.
  • 考问之 - Means 'questioned her under examination,' indicating an interrogation.
  • 修正尚未蒙福，为邪欲以何望 - Means 'I have been virtuous but have not received blessings; what can I hope for by doing evil?' expressing Ban Jieyu's innocence.
  • 使鬼神有知，不受小臣之愬 - Means 'if gods and spirits know, they will not accept the complaints of a low-ranking official,' appealing to divine justice.
  • 如其无知，愬之何益 - Means 'if they do not know, what good would it do to complain?' suggesting futility in complaining to the ignorant.
  • 上善其对，赦之 - Means 'the emperor appreciated her response and pardoned her,' indicating he believed her innocence.
  • 婕妤恐久见危，乃求供养太后于长信宫 - Means 'Jieyu feared prolonged danger and sought to serve the empress dowager at Changxin Palace,' indicating her strategy to avoid further harm.
  • 作《纨扇》诗以自况 - Means 'wrote the 'Silk Fan' poem to describe herself,' indicating a metaphorical self-representation.
  • 新裂齐纨素 - Means 'newly torn silk from Qi,' describing the material of the fan.
  • 皎洁如霜雪 - Means 'bright and white like frost and snow,' describing the purity of the silk.
  • 裁为合欢扇 - Means 'cut into a happy reunion fan,' describing the shape of the fan.
  • 团圆似明月 - Means 'round like the full moon,' further describing the shape.
  • 出入君怀袖 - Means 'in and out of the lord's sleeves,' indicating the fan's presence with the emperor.
  • 动摇微风发 - Means 'when shaken, it emits a gentle breeze,' describing the fan's function.
  • 弃捐箧笥中，恩情中道绝 - Means 'abandoned in a box, affection cut off midway,' indicating the loss of favor.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 班婕妤 - Refers to Ban Jieyu, a historical figure known for her literary works and moral integrity.
  • 成帝 - Refers to Emperor Cheng of Han, who reigned from 33 BC to 7 BC.
  • 飞燕姊弟 - Refers to Zhao Feiyan and her brother Zhao Yiyang, who were favorites of Emperor Cheng and played significant roles in the court.
  • 长信宫 - Refers to Changxin Palace, where the empress dowager lived, indicating Ban Jieyu's move to a safer place.
  • 《纨扇》诗 - Refers to the 'Silk Fan' poem, a famous work by Ban Jieyu that uses the metaphor of a fan to express her feelings about her declining favor.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sequence describes Ban Jieyu's entry into the imperial harem and her rise to a favored position.
  • This sequence shows the emperor's desire and Ban Jieyu's response.
  • This parallel structure contrasts the behavior of virtuous rulers with that of corrupt ones.
  • This rhetorical question emphasizes Ban Jieyu's innocence and the futility of evil actions.
  • This conditional statement appeals to divine justice and the futility of complaining to the ignorant.
  • This parallel structure uses the metaphor of a fan to express the transient nature of favor.
