  • 善為翹袖折腰雲舞 - Skilled in the *qiūxiù zhéyāo yúnwǔ* (flying sleeves, bending waist, cloud dance), a specific type of Han dynasty court dance.
  • 歌出塞入塞望歸之曲 - Singing songs of departure from the frontier and longing for return.
  • 使夫人擊築，歌《大風》詩以和之 - Had the lady play the *zhù* and sing the poem *Dafeng* (Great Wind) to harmonize with it.
  • 我欲易之，彼羽翼已成，難動搖矣 - I want to replace him, but his wings have already grown; it is difficult to shake him.
  • 汝為我楚舞，吾為若楚歌 - You perform for me a dance of Chu, and I will sing for you a song of Chu.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 戚夫人 - A concubine of Emperor Gaozu of Han (Liu Bang), known for her musical and dancing talents.
  • 鼓瑟擊築 - Playing the *sè* (zither) and *zhù* (a small, handheld stringed instrument), both traditional instruments of the Han dynasty.
  • 翹袖折腰雲舞 - A specific Han dynasty court dance, known for its graceful movements and association with the imperial court.
  • 出塞入塞望歸之曲 - Songs that reflect the emotions of soldiers or people on the frontier, longing for home.
  • 高帝 - The honorific title of Liu Bang, the founder of the Han dynasty.
  • 趙王如意 - The son of Liu Bang and戚夫人, who was a rival to the Crown Prince, Liu Ying.
  • 留侯 - The honorific title of Zhang Liang, a key strategist in the Han dynasty.
  • 四皓 - The Four Elders, four reclusive scholars who were persuaded to support the Crown Prince, Liu Ying.
  • 《大風》詩 - A poem by Emperor Gaozu, expressing his reflections on his reign and his loyalty to his subjects.
  • 楚舞 - A style of dance associated with the ancient state of Chu, known for its elegance and emotional intensity.

Proper Nouns

  • 戚夫人 - The name of the concubine.
  • 高帝 - The honorific title of Liu Bang, the founder of the Han dynasty.
  • 趙王如意 - The son of Liu Bang and戚夫人.
  • 留侯 - The honorific title of Zhang Liang.
  • 四皓 - The Four Elders.
  • 《大風》詩 - A poem by Emperor Gaozu.

[597]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 所施 - A general term meaning 'what is done' or 'what is carried out,' often used in a context of action or effect.
  • 永巷 - Refers to a prison for women, specifically the one where the Empress Lü confined Lady Qi.
  • 髡钳 - A form of punishment involving shaving the head and placing a wooden collar around the neck.
  • 衣赭衣 - Wearing a red-dyed cloth, which was a form of humiliating punishment.
  • 令舂 - Forced labor, specifically grinding grain.
  • 相离三千里 - A hyperbolic expression indicating a great distance, symbolizing separation.
  • 当使谁告汝 - A rhetorical question meaning 'who will tell you?' or 'who will inform you?'
  • 人彘之祸 - A euphemism for a severe and cruel punishment, often translated as 'human swine' or 'pig-like punishment.'
  • 愿吕为鼠我为猫 - A curse wishing for a life where one can eat the other, symbolizing a desire for revenge.
  • 鲁、卫之政 - Refers to the political systems of the states of Lu and Wei, often used to describe a stable and moderate governance.
  • 两置之 - A phrase meaning 'to place both,' suggesting a balanced approach.
  • 尽善乎 - A phrase meaning 'perfect' or 'completely good,' often used to express a high standard of excellence.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 高后 - Empress Lü, the regent after Emperor Gaozu's death, known for her strong rule and controversial actions.
  • 戚夫人 - Lady Qi, a concubine of Emperor Gaozu and mother of Prince Yi.
  • 赵王如意 - Prince Yi, the son of Lady Qi and Emperor Gaozu.
  • 鸩之 - To poison, a method used to kill Prince Yi.
  • 四皓 - The Four Sages, respected scholars who supported Liu Bang (Emperor Gaozu) and later advised Emperor Hui.
  • 惠帝 - Emperor Hui, the son of Emperor Gaozu and Empress Lü, known for his weak rule.
  • 文帝 - Emperor Wen, the son of Prince Yi and Lady Qi, who later became emperor.
  • 鲁、卫之政 - Refers to the political systems of the states of Lu and Wei, often used to describe a stable and moderate governance.
  • 举朝 - The entire court, referring to the officials and nobles in the imperial government.
  • 诸悍将大臣 - Powerful generals and ministers, often used to describe influential figures in the court.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 初立妃王氏为后 - Initially established Wang as empress.
  • 已而宠萧淑妃 - Later favored Xiao Shufei.
  • 及武氏入宫为昭仪 - When Wu entered the palace as a zhaoyi.
  • 后与淑妃宠皆衰 - Both the empress and Xiao Shufei lost their favor.
  • 会昭仪生女，后怜而弄之 - When the zhaoyi gave birth to a daughter, the empress felt pity and played with her.
  • 后出，昭仪潜扼杀之 - When the empress left, the zhaoyi secretly strangled her.
  • 上至，昭仪佯欢笑 - When the emperor arrived, the zhaoyi feigned happiness.
  • 发被视女已死矣 - When they uncovered the child, they saw she was already dead.
  • 即惊啼，问左右 - They immediately cried out in shock and asked the attendants.
  • 左右曰：“皇后适来此。” - The attendants said, 'The empress just came here.'
  • 上怒曰：“后杀吾女。” - The emperor became angry and said, 'The empress killed my daughter.'
  • 昭仪因谮之 - The zhaoyi then slandered her.
  • 后遂与淑妃并废为庶人，囚于别苑 - The empress and Xiao Shufei were then both deposed and imprisoned in a separate garden.
  • 而立武氏为后 - And Wu was made empress.
  • 上一日念后，间行至囚所 - One day, the emperor thought of the empress and secretly went to her prison.
  • 见门禁锢严，进饮食窦中 - He saw that the door was tightly locked and that food was being passed through a small opening.
  • 恻然伤之 - He was moved with compassion.
  • 呼曰：“皇后、良姊无恙？” - He called out, 'Empress, good sister, are you well?'
  • 二人同辞曰：“妾等非罪弃为婢，安得尊称邪？” - Both replied, 'We were wrongfully cast aside as maids, how can we deserve such respect?'
  • 因流涕呜咽 - They wept and sobbed.
  • 又曰：“至尊若念畴昔，使得见日月，乞署此为回心院。” - They also said, 'If Your Majesty remembers our past, allow us to see the sun and moon again, and please name this place the Heart-Return Pavilion.'
  • 上曰：“朕即有处置。” - The emperor said, 'I will take care of it.'
  • 武氏闻之，大怒，遣人断去手足，投酒瓮中 - Wu heard of this and became very angry, ordering someone to cut off their hands and feet and throw them into a wine jar.
  • 曰：“令二妪骨醉。” - Saying, 'Let the bones of the two women be drunk.'
  • 后数见二人为祟 - The empress often saw the two women haunting her.
  • 故多居洛阳，不敢归长安 - Therefore, she often stayed in Luoyang and did not dare return to Chang'an.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 高宗 - Refers to Emperor Gaozong of Tang, the ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 唐王后 - Refers to the empress of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 萧淑妃 - Refers to Xiao Shufei, one of the concubines of Emperor Gaozong.
  • 武氏 - Refers to Wu Zetian, who later became the only female emperor in Chinese history.
  • 昭仪 - A rank in the imperial harem, indicating a high status.
  • 庶人 - Refers to commoners or ordinary people, often used to describe those who have been deposed from noble status.
  • 别苑 - Refers to a separate garden or palace, often used for imprisonment or isolation.
  • 回心院 - Refers to a place named Heart-Return Pavilion, symbolizing a place of reflection and repentance.
  • 洛阳 - Refers to Luoyang, an important city in Chinese history.
  • 长安 - Refers to Chang'an, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 高宗与汉高帝不同 - Comparative statement indicating that Emperor Gaozong (唐高宗) and Emperor Gaozu of Han (汉高祖刘邦) differ in character or approach.
  • 一步百计 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'every step involves numerous strategies,' highlighting the cunning and strategic nature of Emperor Gaozu.
  • 欲割小爱以就大事 - Means 'to sacrifice minor loves for the sake of major causes,' emphasizing the willingness to prioritize larger goals over personal attachments.
  • 杂情奴才 - Literally means 'a servant of mixed emotions,' implying a person who is indecisive or easily swayed by various feelings.
  • 一味怕婆而已 - Colloquial expression meaning 'nothing more than being afraid of one's wife,' suggesting a lack of courage or decisiveness.
  • 见其少丽 - Means 'seeing her young and beautiful,' describing the initial impression of the young lady.
  • 选归侍明皇 - Means 'chosen to serve Emperor Ming,' referring to her selection to enter the imperial court.
  • 视如尘土 - Means 'to regard as dust,' indicating a sense of disdain or disregard.
  • 宫中亦自以为不及 - Means 'even the palace women thought they were not as good,' highlighting the high regard for the lady's beauty and charm.
  • 性喜梅 - Means 'she loved plum blossoms,' indicating her preference for this flower.
  • 榜曰“梅亭” - Means 'named it the Plum Pavilion,' describing the naming of the pavilion.
  • 赋赏至夜分，尚顾恋花下不能去 - Means 'enjoyed the plum blossoms until midnight, still reluctant to leave,' emphasizing her deep appreciation for the flowers.
  • 戏名曰“梅妃” - Means 'playfully named her the Plum Princess,' indicating the emperor's affectionate nickname for her.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 高宗 - Refers to Emperor Gaozong of Tang (唐高宗李治), the third emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 汉高帝 - Refers to Emperor Gaozu of Han (汉高祖刘邦), the founder of the Han Dynasty.
  • 明皇 - Refers to Emperor Ming of Tang (唐明皇李隆基), the fourth emperor of the Tang Dynasty and a famous poet.
  • 梅妃 - Refers to Princess Mei, a historical figure known for her beauty and love of plum blossoms.
  • 江氏 - The surname of the princess, indicating her family background.
  • 莆田 - A city in Fujian Province, China, where the princess was born.
  • 父仲逊 - The father of the princess, whose name is Jiang Zhongxun.
  • 世为医 - Means 'his family has been physicians for generations,' indicating the family's profession.
  • 九岁 - Refers to the age of nine, a significant age in Chinese culture often associated with childhood.
  • 二南 - Refers to the 'Two Nan,' a section of the Book of Songs (诗经) that includes poems from the regions of Nan and Bei.
  • 高力士 - A famous eunuch who served several Tang emperors, including Emperor Ming.
  • 笄 - Refers to the coming-of-age ceremony for girls in ancient China, typically around the age of 15.
  • 大内、大明、兴庆三宫 - Refers to the three main palaces in the Tang Dynasty capital: Daming Palace, Daming Palace, and Xingqing Palace.
  • 东都大内、上阳两宫 - Refers to the two main palaces in the eastern capital: Daming Palace and Shangyang Palace.
  • 四万人 - Refers to the large number of palace women, indicating the grandeur of the imperial court.

Proper Nouns

  • 高宗 - Emperor Gaozong of Tang (唐高宗李治)
  • 汉高帝 - Emperor Gaozu of Han (汉高祖刘邦)
  • 明皇 - Emperor Ming of Tang (唐明皇李隆基)
  • 梅妃 - Princess Mei (梅妃江采蘋)
  • 江氏 - The surname of the princess
  • 莆田 - A city in Fujian Province, China
  • 父仲逊 - The father of the princess, Jiang Zhongxun
  • 世为医 - The family's profession as physicians
  • 九岁 - The age of nine
  • 二南 - The 'Two Nan' section of the Book of Songs (诗经)
  • 高力士 - A famous eunuch who served several Tang emperors
  • 笄 - The coming-of-age ceremony for girls in ancient China
  • 大内、大明、兴庆三宫 - The three main palaces in the Tang Dynasty capital
  • 东都大内、上阳两宫 - The two main palaces in the eastern capital

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是时承平岁久，海内无事 - At this time, the country had enjoyed long-term peace and stability, with no major troubles at home.
  • 上于兄弟间极友爱 - The emperor was extremely affectionate towards his brothers.
  • 日从燕间，必妃侍侧 - Every day during leisurely gatherings, he would always have his concubines by his side.
  • 上命破橙往赐诸王 - The emperor ordered someone to deliver broken oranges to the princes. '破橙' (broken oranges) is likely a metaphorical expression, possibly symbolizing something of lesser value or a gesture of humility.
  • 至汉邸，潜以足蹑妃履，登时退阁 - When he arrived at Prince Han's residence, he secretly stepped on the concubine's shoe with his foot, causing her to immediately retreat into the inner chambers. The action of stepping on the shoe is a subtle and indirect way of sending a message.
  • 上命连趣，报言“适履珠脱缀，缀竟当来” - The emperor repeatedly urged her, and she replied, 'Just now, the pearl on my shoe came loose; I will come as soon as it is fixed.' The response is a clever excuse to delay her appearance.
  • 后上与妃斗茶，顾诸王戏曰 - Later, the emperor and the concubine engaged in a tea competition, and the emperor, looking at the princes, playfully said...
  • 此‘梅精’也，吹白玉笛，作惊鸿舞，一座光辉 - This is the 'Plum Spirit'; she plays the white jade flute and performs the startled swan dance, making the entire gathering radiant. The term '梅精' (Plum Spirit) is a metaphorical reference to the concubine's exceptional beauty and talent.
  • 斗茶今又胜我矣 - She has once again won in the tea competition.
  • 草木之戏，误胜陛下 - It's just a game with plants; I accidentally won against Your Majesty.
  • 设使调和四海，烹饪鼎鼐，万乘自有心法，贱妾何能较胜负也 - If it were a matter of governing the world and cooking in the imperial kitchen, the emperor has his own methods; how could a lowly concubine compare?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 承平岁久，海内无事 - This reflects the period of peace and stability during the reign of Emperor Huizong of Song, a time when the Song Dynasty was relatively prosperous and free from major internal conflicts.
  • 上于兄弟间极友爱 - The emperor's affection for his brothers is a common theme in Chinese imperial history, reflecting the importance of familial bonds in the imperial court.
  • 斗茶 - Tea competitions were a popular pastime among the literati and nobility during the Song Dynasty, often involving elaborate rituals and displays of skill.
  • 梅精 - The term '梅精' (Plum Spirit) is a poetic reference to a beautiful and talented woman, often used in literature to describe concubines or courtesans.
  • 白玉笛 - The white jade flute is a symbol of elegance and refinement, often associated with high culture and artistic pursuits.
  • 惊鸿舞 - The startled swan dance is a metaphor for graceful and elegant movements, often used to describe the beauty of a dancer or performer.

Proper Nouns

  • 上 - Refers to the emperor, likely Emperor Huizong of Song.
  • 妃 - Refers to the concubine, likely Consort Li or another favored concubine.
  • 汉邸 - Refers to Prince Han's residence, indicating a specific location within the imperial palace.
  • 梅精 - A metaphorical reference to the concubine, emphasizing her beauty and talent.
  • 白玉笛 - A symbolic object representing elegance and refinement.
  • 惊鸿舞 - A metaphorical dance, symbolizing grace and beauty.

[601]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 会太真杨氏入侍 - When Yang Guifei entered the service, where '会' indicates the timing of an event, and '入侍' means 'entered the service' or 'joined the imperial household.'
  • 宠爱日夺 - Daily taking away the favor, where '日夺' suggests a gradual or incremental loss of favor.
  • 无疏意 - No intention to neglect, where '无' negates the verb, and '疏意' means 'intention to neglect.'
  • 避路而行 - Avoided the road and walked, where '避路' can be interpreted as 'avoiding the main road' or 'stepping aside,' and '而行' means 'and walked.'
  • 上尝方之英、皇 - The emperor once compared them to Emperor Gaozu and Emperor Wu, where '尝' means 'once,' '方' means 'compared,' and '英、皇' refers to Emperor Gaozu (Liu Bang) and Emperor Wu (Liu Che).
  • 议者谓广狭不类 - Those who discussed it said that their scope was not similar, where '议者' means 'those who discussed,' '谓' means 'said,' and '广狭不类' means 'their scope was not similar.'
  • 窃笑之 - Laughed at it secretly, where '窃' means 'secretly' or 'stealthily,' and '笑之' means 'laughed at it.'
  • 太真忌而智 - Yang Guifei was jealous and intelligent, where '忌' means 'jealous,' and '智' means 'intelligent.'
  • 妃性柔缓，亡以胜 - The empress was gentle and slow, unable to compete, where '性柔缓' means 'gentle and slow,' and '亡以胜' means 'unable to compete.'
  • 后竟为杨氏迁于上阳东宫 - Later, she was moved by Yang Guifei to the East Palace of Shangyang, where '后竟' means 'later,' '为杨氏迁' means 'moved by Yang Guifei,' and '上阳东宫' refers to the East Palace of Shangyang.
  • 上忆妃 - The emperor remembered the empress, where '忆' means 'remembered.'
  • 夜遣小黄门灭烛 - Sent a small yellow door official to extinguish the candle, where '夜' means 'at night,' '遣' means 'sent,' '小黄门' refers to a low-ranking palace official, and '灭烛' means 'extinguish the candle.'
  • 密以戏马召妃至翠华西阁 - Secretly summoned the empress to the West Pavilion of Cuihua with a horse, where '密' means 'secretly,' '以' means 'with,' '戏马' refers to a horse, and '翠华西阁' is the West Pavilion of Cuihua.
  • 叙旧爱，悲不自胜 - Recalled past love, feeling so sad that he couldn't bear it, where '叙旧爱' means 'recalled past love,' and '悲不自胜' means 'feeling so sad that he couldn't bear it.'
  • 继而上失寤 - Afterward, the emperor fell asleep, where '继而' means 'afterward,' and '失寤' means 'fell asleep.'
  • 侍御惊报曰 - The attendant was startled and reported, saying, where '侍御' means 'attendant,' '惊报' means 'was startled and reported,' and '曰' means 'said.'
  • 妃子已届阁前，将奈何 - The empress has already arrived at the pavilion, what shall we do? where '届阁前' means 'has already arrived at the pavilion,' and '将奈何' means 'what shall we do?'
  • 乞宣至，今日同浴温泉 - Requested her to be summoned, to bathe in the hot springs together today, where '乞宣至' means 'requested her to be summoned,' and '同浴温泉' means 'to bathe in the hot springs together.'
  • 此女已放屏，无并往也 - This woman has already been dismissed, there is no need to go together, where '放屏' means 'dismissed,' and '无并往也' means 'there is no need to go together.'
  • 太真语益坚 - Yang Guifei spoke more insistently, where '语益坚' means 'spoke more insistently.'
  • 上顾左右不答 - The emperor looked around and did not respond, where '顾左右' means 'looked around,' and '不答' means 'did not respond.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太真杨氏 - Refers to Yang Guifei, one of the most famous consorts of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, known for her beauty and influence.
  • 上 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a powerful and influential ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 英、皇 - Refers to Emperor Gaozu (Liu Bang) and Emperor Wu (Liu Che), two of the most significant emperors of the Han Dynasty.
  • 上阳东宫 - Refers to the East Palace of Shangyang, a palace in the imperial city of Chang'an.
  • 翠华西阁 - Refers to the West Pavilion of Cuihua, a specific location within the imperial palace.
  • 小黄门 - Refers to a low-ranking palace official, often responsible for minor tasks and errands.
  • 戏马 - Refers to a horse, often used in imperial contexts for ceremonial or symbolic purposes.
  • 温泉 - Refers to hot springs, which were often used for bathing and relaxation in imperial settings.

Proper Nouns

  • 太真杨氏 - Yang Guifei
  • 上 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang
  • 英、皇 - Emperor Gaozu (Liu Bang) and Emperor Wu (Liu Che)
  • 上阳东宫 - East Palace of Shangyang
  • 翠华西阁 - West Pavilion of Cuihua
  • 小黄门 - Low-ranking palace official
  • 戏马 - Horse
  • 温泉 - Hot springs

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 肴核狼藉 - Food and fruit were scattered in disorder, describing a chaotic scene.
  • 恐怜我则动肥婢情，岂非弃也 - If he loves me, he will be moved by the feelings of the fat servant, isn't that abandonment? A cynical comment by the concubine.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太真 - Refers to Yang Guifei, the favorite concubine of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, known for her beauty and influence.
  • 御榻 - Refers to the imperial bed, a symbol of the emperor's authority and privacy.
  • 小黄门 - Refers to a low-ranking eunuch, often responsible for minor tasks in the imperial court.
  • 东宫 - Refers to the eastern palace, where the emperor's concubines lived.
  • 高力士 - A powerful eunuch who served Emperor Xuanzong and had significant influence in the court.
  • 司马相如 - A famous Han dynasty poet known for his romantic and literary works.
  • 《长门赋》 - A famous essay by Sima Xiangru, written for the former empress Chen Jiao, who had been demoted to the Longmen Palace.
  • 《楼东赋》 - An essay written by Yang Guifei, likely inspired by the style and themes of 'The Longmen Palace.'

  • 东宫 - The eastern palace.
  • 高力士 - A powerful eunuch who served Emperor Xuanzong.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 玉鉴尘生 - Symbolizes neglect or desolation, literally 'dust gathers on the jade mirror'.
  • 凤奁香殄 - Indicates the loss of beauty or vitality, literally 'fragrance has faded from the phoenix casket'.
  • 懒蝉之巧梳 - Suggests a lack of effort in grooming, literally 'skillful combing by a lazy cicada'.
  • 闲缕衣之轻练 - Indicates a lack of activity or care, literally 'leisurely woven light silk clothes'.
  • 苦寂寞于蕙宫 - Indicates a place of solitude, literally 'suffering from loneliness in the fragrant palace'.
  • 但凝思乎兰殿 - Indicates a place of contemplation, literally 'only thinking intently in the fragrant hall'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太真 - Reference to Yang Guifei, one of the most famous concubines of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.

  • 江妃 - Reference to Jiang Qing, a concubine of Emperor Xuanzong often compared to Yang Guifei.
  • 岭表使 - Messenger from the southern regions, indicating a long distance.
  • 庶邦贡杨妃果实（荔）使来 - Messenger bringing fruits (likely lychees) as a tribute, indicating the emperor's favor towards Yang Guifei.
  • 长门 - Reference to the long gate, often associated with the palace, symbolizing separation and isolation.

Proper Nouns

  • 江妃 - Jiang Qing.
  • 岭表使 - Messenger from the southern regions.
  • 长门 - The long gate, often associated with the palace.

  • 上览诗，怅然不乐 - The emperor read the poem, and was deeply saddened.

  • 号《一斛珠》 - And it was named 'One Measure of Pearls.'
  • 后禄山犯阙 - Later, An Lushan rebelled against the imperial court.
  • 上西幸 - The emperor fled westward.
  • 太真死 - Eternal Beauty (Yang Guifei) died.
  • 及东归 - When he returned eastward.
  • 寻妃所在，不可得 - He sought Yang Guifei, but could not find her.
  • 上悲，谓兵火之后，流落他处 - The emperor was sad and believed she had been lost in the chaos of war.
  • 访搜不知所在 - But despite searching, no one knew where she was.
  • 上又命方士飞神御气 - The emperor then ordered a Daoist practitioner to use spiritual techniques.
  • 潜经天地，亦不可得 - To search the heavens and earth, but still could not find her.
  • 有宦者进其画真 - A eunuch presented a portrait of her.
  • 诗题于上 - The poem was inscribed on the portrait.
  • ‘忆昔娇妃在紫宸，铅华不御得天真。霜绡虽似当时态，争奈娇波不顾人。’ - It reads: 'Remembering the time when the beautiful concubine was in the Purple Palace, without makeup, she revealed her natural beauty. The white silk still resembles her appearance then, but how could her charming gaze ignore me now?'
  • 读之泣下 - Reading it, he wept.
  • 命模像刊石 - He ordered a stone carving made in her likeness.
  • 后上暑月昼寝 - Later, during a hot summer month, the emperor took a daytime nap.
  • 仿佛见妃隔竹间泣 - He seemed to see Yang Guifei weeping behind a bamboo screen.
  • 含涕障袂，如花朦雾露状 - Her tears blurred her face, like a flower shrouded in mist and dew.
  • 上骇然流汗而寤 - The emperor was startled, broke out in a sweat, and woke up.
  • 登时令往太液池发视之 - He immediately ordered them to dig at the Taiye Pool.
  • 无获 - But they found nothing.
  • 上益不乐 - The emperor became even more sad.
  • 忽悟温泉汤池侧有梅十余株 - Suddenly, he realized there were more than ten plum trees by the hot springs pool.
  • 岂在是乎 - Could she be there?
  • 上自命驾 - The emperor personally went there.
  • 令发视 - He ordered them to dig.
  • 才数株，得尸 - After digging a few trees, they found her body.
  • 裹以锦褥，盛以酒槽，附土三尺许 - She was wrapped in a brocade quilt and placed in a wine vat, with three feet of soil covering her.
  • 上大恸 - The emperor wept bitterly.
  • 左右莫能仰视 - No one could look at him.
  • 视其所伤，肋下有刀痕 - When they examined her, there was a knife wound on her ribs.
  • 上自制文诔之 - The emperor wrote an elegy for her.
  • 以妃礼易葬焉 - And she was buried with the rites due to a concubine.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太真 - The nickname of Yang Guifei, one of the Four Great Beauties of ancient China, who was a favorite concubine of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 禄山 - An Lushan, a military general who led a rebellion against the Tang Dynasty.
  • 紫宸 - The Purple Palace, a metaphor for the imperial court.
  • 铅华不御 - Refers to Yang Guifei's natural beauty, unadorned by makeup.
  • 霜绡 - A metaphor for Yang Guifei's skin, comparing it to frosty silk.
  • 娇波 - Refers to Yang Guifei's charming gaze.
  • 蒙尘 - A euphemism for the emperor being forced to flee due to rebellion or invasion.
  • 太液池 - A large pond in the imperial palace complex.
  • 温泉汤池 - A hot springs pool, often associated with luxury and leisure in imperial settings.
  • 锦褥 - A brocade quilt, a luxurious bedding material.
  • 酒槽 - A wine vat, used here as a container for burial.
  • 妃礼 - The rites and ceremonies due to a concubine of the emperor.

Proper Nouns

  • 一斛珠 - The name of the poem.
  • 紫宸 - The Purple Palace, a metaphor for the imperial court.
  • 太液池 - A large pond in the imperial palace complex.
  • 温泉汤池 - A hot springs pool, often associated with luxury and leisure in imperial settings.
